boardgame: Cadwallon: City of Thieves – The King of Ashes
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Thunder splits the sky as dark clouds gather above the free city of Cadwallon. The ground trembles, towers sway, and an earthquake of unprecedented violence wracks the City of Thieves! In the midst of the crisis, as bystanders flee the filthy streets, an entrance opens to a series of long-forgotten catacombs.

The guild intrigues of Cadwallon: City of Thieves grow even richer with the treasure trove of new adventures, characters, equipment, and Arcana in The King of Ashes expansion!

Rumors claim the newly-opened catacombs contain the legendary treasure of Sophet Drahas, and the thieves of the city above race to find the entrance to these long-hidden catacombs and grab their riches. The King of Ashes explores these catacombs with a new board and six adventures that can be played independently or combined into a larger campaign.

Revised rules make the militia a more imposing force, and rules for experience, equipment, and mercenaries afford tremendous strategic options in your games, especially when you play them as part of a larger campaign.

boardgame: Siebzehn: The Race of All Races
description:
Siebzehn, the number 17 in the German language, is the ultimate competition – The Race of All Races. Nine different races of mythical creatures compete for supremacy. Each mythical race is represented by a team consisting of six numbered pieces. Two to nine players control the teams, which maneuver around the board in a counterclockwise fashion. The rules of Siebzehn are simple, but the continuous discovery of new strategies amidst the constantly evolving environment is complex, captivating and magical.

Each team proceeds such that a higher number piece can never pass a lower number piece, and the number 1 piece advances only if no other team pieces can move. Pieces move forward and diagonally in the straightaways and forward only in the turns. The object of the game is to eliminate other races and control the one race that eventually conquers all. Players eliminate opponents from the board by one of three methods:


    Circumnavigate the board and pass the highest number piece of one or more opponents with the 1 piece of one of your teams. This is known as a cataclysm and some teams will be eliminated or altered.
    Pass an opponent's 1 piece with the highest numbered piece of one of your teams. In other words, one team passes an opponent's entire team. The team that is passed is eliminated.
    Control the most advanced 1 piece in games that reach the end of the 17th cycle, known as the siebzehn cycle.


The concept of a race around a large track is central to the essence of Siebzehn, but the game is not a model of any known race. Several racing games involve familiar competitors such as horses, chariots or cars – then there is Siebzehn, an imaginary mystical race in which the competitors are famed creatures of myth and legend. As the teams travel around the board, they must persevere through careful movement and placement, calculated blocking of their own and opponent pieces, opponent blocking effects and injuries, dice probability considerations and consequences, and new unexpected ways of seeing the board.

For an inspiring and exciting gaming experience, discover the land far beyond the familiar and influence the fates of mythical creatures in Siebzehn: The Race of All Races!

boardgame: Perfect Word: A Word Dice Game
description:
Unlike other word games, the objective of Perfect Word is to derive the single word that best matches (4) types of criteria. Roll (12) dice, draw one Perfect Word card and start the 2-minute timer. Use as many point letters as possible, including non-point letters as needed. Perfect Word cards permit one helpful exception, such as unlimited license to use the letter "P".

Word games are more fun when there is a challenging twist. The longest words are not necessarily the Perfect Word. One point is deducted for every syllable. If the required letters are available, "squirreled" is a Perfect Word. It contains (10) letters but only has (1) syllable.

Absorb your enemy, but don't get eaten by a Grue. An abstract strategy game for Looney pyramids of capture and maneuver.

From the Icehousegames.org background:
"Battles on the savage gleeb homeworld are fought in deadly arenas. Two groups of gleebs enter, each aiming to absorb the enemy before meeting that grisly fate themselves. Further adding to the excitement are three ravenous grues who have their own plan for the gleebs..."

Each player takes 2 colors of pyramids, while the black pyramids represent Grues. On their turn, players move one of their pyramids onto a pyramid of a different color, and then must move a black pyramid, potentially immobilizing or removing pyramids from the game, even their own.

The player who controls the most pips worth of pyramids at the end of the game wins.

Requires a 4x4 playing board and 1 Treehouse stash:
3 blue pyramids in small, medium, and large
3 red pyramids in small, medium, and large
3 yellow pyramids in small, medium, and large
3 green pyramids in small, medium, and large
3 black pyramids in small, medium, and large
for a total of 15 pyramids.

You can play Battle of the Sexes 2 in two variants, either as a team game, "women against men" or as individual players' every man for himself.

There are fewer chips than first version of the game.

In his turn the team roll the dice what determines the question to be asked. There are five questions and their answers on each card. If "6" is rolled the random question is chosen.
They reads out a question that must now discuss the other team, offered possible answers which could well be the correct or may be bluff. Other team must answer correctly to move their pawn accordinglyon the game board.
Team who finish first is the winner.

description:
In this game players use the four basic arithmetic operations. The aim of the game is, with at least three of the dice to calculate the numbers on the previously required results from the drawn cards.

The card is drawn the dice are rolled and the quickest player socres a point.

This is an operational level game covering the France Campaign, May - October 1940.  Units are primarily divisions, with air formations also provided.  There are 1,152 die-cut units representing French, British, Belgian, Dutch, German and Italian forces.  The 32" x 22" inch map runs from the North Sea to the Mediterranean Seas.  The War in France & Flanders was to be part of larger series of games called Crusade in Europe, which never appeared.

The sequence of play is I-Go-You-Go.  There are detailed rules covering air operations; alternatively, players can use a simplified set of abstract air rules.  Victory is determined by points awarded for losses, and for the date of any French surrender.

In his turn player rolls dice and a card is drawn where the question is asked according to dice-roll. If the answer is correct the player advances his pawn one step further.
there are also some colored barriers on the game board what need only the correct answers otherwise the pawns moves back one step.

The player who finishes first on the board is the winner.

The game contains 3300 questions and answer choices from the varied experience and experience range from pre-school children.
In his turn player rolls the die and draws a card where the question is read according to die roll. There are four possible answers for each question. Player gets the card if the answer is correct.

At the end of the game the player with the most cards is the winner.

The game contains 3300 questions and answer choices from the varied experience and experience range from pre-school children.
In his turn player rolls the die and draws a card where the question is read according to die roll. There are four possible answers for each question. Player gets the card if the answer is correct.

At the end of the game the player with the most cards is the winner.

Help to help in English.
Players are volunteers who try to help and support people who need clothes, food, medicine, work or money.
In his turn player roll the dice and move up to 4 volunteers of his team on the game board to search sponsors and needed resources. If then player is able to support one or more people/organizations he scores a point for every such act.

Player who have the most points by supporting at the end of the game is the winner.

Each player tries to complete a picture of a fish, made up of 9 tiles. Some tiles are placed openly on the table, most are facedown. In his/her turn, a player can either take an open tile and place it in his/her picture, or flip open three tiles and take one of these (if it fits into his/her picture; the tile has to be adjacent to a previously placed one). After all tiles have been seen once, a player can either take one open tile or a facedown one (that s/he has remembered from a previous turn). The first player to finish his/her picture is the winner. 
The game is made difficult by the fact that the two pictures are mirror images of each other, so it isn't always obvious which picture a tile belongs to. There are four fish pictures included, so two or four players can play.

The game consists of tiles showing postage stamps. The tiles are shuffled and placed on the table in a grid, leaving some spaces free. Each player gets a secret collector's interest tile, showing which kind of stamps s/he collects. 
In a turn, the active player picks up a tile, shows it to everyone and places it facedown in one of the free spaces in the grid. This continues until all tiles have been flipped over. In subsequent turns, a player can either swap two facedown tiles, move a facedown tile to an empty space or flip open a tile, putting it back to where it was. 
When a player thinks that there are five stamps of his/her field of interest in a row, s/he flips these open to check. If correct, points are scored. Other players get as many points as "their" tiles are in a row as well. 
The player who scored most points after a certain number of rounds is the winner.

A simple "action" game for two players. Each player controls a WWI plastic biplane via a control rod mechanism. The control rods cause the biplanes to fly circles in opposite directions. It appears that players attempt to unseat each others planes from the control rods, socring points when doing so.

The game features two biplanes molded in red and yellow, a cardboard playing surface with a WWI "Battlefield" printed on it, two plastic "tents" which serve as the base for the plane/control rod mechanism, scoring tracks and markers, control rods and decals.

Armis is a two player high-strategy board game, your objective is to capture the opponent's flag.  Success is achieved by planning, implementing, and assessing various strategies and tactics given specific rules, resources, and environments.

Armis rules are detailed in the Player’s Handbook; the game has three environments: land, air, and sea;  and the 17 resources are: Flag, President, Vice President, Army, Aircraft Carrier, Submarine, Jet, Helicopter, Marines, Coast Guard, Reserve, Diplomat, Religion, Child, Mover, Media, and Nuke.

Given the above, players instinctively and continuously formulate offensive and defensive situations until a winner emerges.

Armis is great exercise for the mind, and the brain.

boardgame: Unser Sandmännchen: Komm mit ins Traumland
description:
In this game about sandman, players visit foreign countries with him: India, Hawaii or Mexico etc. 
All tiles are shuffled and placed face down on the table. In his turn player turns up a tile. If the background is dark, he can either take all face-up tiles from the table and ends his turn or he can turns up another tile, but when the sandman appears on the tile the next player starts his turn. The upturned tiles stay in place for next players. When all sandmen are turned up, the player with the most travel tiles wins.

In Variant 3 the players piece is moved, then tiles are searched and depending on the result the player’s piece or the turn stone is moved. The first player to reach the finish wins the game.

There is also possible to bet to boxers You think to win.

The ranking is determined by dice the fighting with the boxing cards and boxing dice.

The game is played over an entire season, the victory decide the highest revenue.

The player must find the missing pages of the storybook in the enchanted forest again. 
In his turn player rolls and moves and looks at the plate next to the target field. If it shows desired fairy tale, You get the card and a new card is revealed. If all the fairy tales have found, the player with the most cards wins.

Players are collecting interesting themes for the next show in the series, there are 6 categories - Space, Dinosaurs, Technology, Underwater, Nature and Man. On the board theme- and action cards are randomly distributed. In his turn a player must answer a question from one of the 6 categories. If his answer is correct, he may move up to three spaces, if his answer is wrong, he moves one space. If his move ends on a space with a card, the player may turn the card over - if he can use it for one of his themes, he can take it. If it is an action card, he can use it immediately or later in the game. The player who gathers his 4 themes for the show first, wins the game..

Promotional game from BP, so naturally it is a game to play in a car. Each player gets a sheet of things to look out for and the one who has seen the most during the journey, wins.

On the reverse of the player sheets there are some picture puzzles, a maze and, bizarrely, a guide to giving mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.

description:
Two opposing fleets of 5 ships each, one fleet being blue and the other red vie to see which fleet can capture all the ships of the other.
A die roll determines which fleet begins in Cadiz and which fleet begins in the Tortugas.
Players use a spinner to determine how far to move. That movement can be in any direction over GOLD roads or over the roads of his/her color. The winner is the player having any ships left, after all of the ships of the enemy have been captured.

boardgame: Game of Discovering the North Pole
description:
This McLoughlin Brothers game comes from 1909.

The Object of the game is to see which player can first reach the North Pole and get home safely.

Each player using his/her colored marker must use that colored space indicator and must obey any directions on places he lands on.

Eventually players will reach a gold spot where all colored roads meet, this does not have to be by exact count. Once reaching that space players may use squares for the remainder of their attempt once again following any directions with an objective of landing on the square marked "North Pole"

Once there, any route can be used to return. If you land on another player piece that piece must be moved back 3 spaces or if that space is occupied to the next available vacant space.

bodyIQ - Premium is a game about the body, how it works, how to improve it and physical exercises.

Basically it a trivia game with a twist. There are 12 categories. 10 based on some of the bodys most important organs, 1 health check and 1 physical excersise, 600 questions/task all in all. Each turn the player roll the die, and moves his marker along the paths of the body. Depending on the space the player ends on, he will either get a question, a health check or a physical task to do. If he answers correct, or performs the task, he will win the card, giving him a number of years.

For younger players there is a multriple choise option.

There are three suggestend ways to end the game:
1) Play until one player reaches 100 years.
2) Play until one player collects all 12 categories.
3) Play for a certaing amount of time.
Whoever has the most points (=years) at the end of the game wins.

boardgame: Age of Steam Expansion: Tibet and Cyprus
description:
Age of Steam: Tibet and Cyprus is another pair of expansions for the Age of Steam board game, with new maps and new rules for each setting.

In Tibet, players may enroll sherpa discs to help them deliver cubes through otherwise impassable mountains. The original printing was for 3-5 players. The 2020 revised edition is for three players only.

In Cyprus, a three-player only map, one player will play the Greeks, another the Turks, and the third the UNO (United Nations Organization) forces. Each player has his own particular strengths and weaknesses.

boardgame: Age of Steam Expansion: Las Vegas and Korea (N&S)
description:
Age of Steam: Las Vegas and Korea (N&S) is another pair of expansions for the Age of Steam board game, with new maps and new rules for each setting.

In Las Vegas, players visit the famed gambling town, trying to build their network as usual while also paying attention to the money on the board.

In Korea (N&S), the cost to build track varies depending on which side of Korea players build upon – but in the wealthy South, there are more cubes to deliver than in the poor North. Delivery between North and South becomes more and more difficult as the game progresses.

boardgame: Super Dungeon Explore: Herald of Vulcanis
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Promotional Anti-Hero for the Dungeon player to be used with Caverns of Roxor.  Each level expansion for Super Dungeon Explore will feature its own Hero or Anti-Hero.


Lord Vulcanis was cast from the marble halls of Celestia due to his fiery temper and bellicose nature. Since his fall he has focused his considerable might on destroying the Celestials’ realm.

Filled with fury at his continued failure he has sent his Heralds across Crystalia to rally the dark races to his banner and subjugate those who would oppose him.

Contents of the box:
1 Fully Assembled Herald of Vulcanis Miniature - 1x Herald of Vulcanis Game Card - 1x Unique Herald of Vulcanis Treasure Card.

v2

A game within the Dablo(t) family. Related to the Sami boardgame Dablot Prejjesne and to Dablo Daares.

Theme: Abstract game with soldiers and kings

Gameplay: Turnbased checkers type of game

Goal of the game: Either to capture the enemy king or reduce his forces so that only the king remains

Rules: All pieces move by single steps and can capture, by the short leap, in all directions. The soldier ("dårake") can only step in the three forward directions. Promotion does not occur. For the soldier, capture is mandatory. For the king ("gånneka") capture is optional. One can only capture a piece of the same rank or lower. Where the king is of higher rank than the soldier.

A piece moves to an empty adjacent point. If an adjacent point is occupied by an enemy piece, of the same rank or lower, and the point directly behind is vacant, then one may jump over it and capture it, as in checkers. Several pieces may be captured like this in a single turn. Dablo Frostviken follows the same rules. For soldiers a capture is mandatory. For the king a capture is optional.
family: Traditional Games: Alquerque / Fanorona (Indian War Games)

description: A card game played with a standard 52-card deck and a mat that outline potential card plays.  Players take turns placing cards out on the display mat, scoring melds of 3 cards as they do.  On a single card play, it's possible to play two melds, with face cards acting as multipliers.

boardgame: Phantoms of the Ice: Expansion Card Set
description:
This is a 100 card expansion for Phantoms of the Ice. It includes 25 offense and 19 defense players as well as 11 new goalies and 42 cards of playing money and 3 position card, one each for goalie, offense, and defense. There is no box and no rules.

Initially this was planned to be released as a new version of Phantoms of the Ice called Power Play and published by Fun Connection. There were new rules for trading and a player market. Although Fun Connections decided to stop producing anymore games, White Wind was able to get the cards printed with their logo on the back and sold those 100 cards as an expansion sets.

ULTRA STARFIRE is the fifth edition of the Starfire game system. Continuing improvements on the basic Starfire combat system, Ultra Starfire is comprised of two parts: the tactical combat system and the campaign system.

The tactical combat system is a fast-paced fleet-level war game of starship combat, still using the same simple combat system designed back in 1976. The campaign system is an empire-level economic war game where the player starts with an alien race on a single world and attempts to conquer the galaxy. Battles from the campaign system are played-out using the tactical system.

Ultra Starfire is a partial rewrite to Galactic Starfire, Fourth edition. Ultra Starfire was designed for completely balanced game play in a player versus player competitive environment. While it could still be used for story-based games, the appendixes and additional material was designed for shorter competitive campaigns.

Ultra Starfire was the first all-electronic product from the SDS, sold as a set of cross-linked PDF files, but still used the counters and map sets from Galactic Starfire.

Re-implements:

     Galactic Starfire

description:
Game consists of 10 numbered dice and two spotted dice.

In a turn, the ten numbered dice are thrown and then the two spotted dice. From the ten dice you may take any number of them that total the number rolled with the spotted dice.
i.e. you roll 9 with the spotted dice: you can now take away any combination of the numbered dice that add up to 9.
If successful, you throw again, and so on.

When you cannot take way the number rolled, the total of the remaing dice are added together and that is your score.

The player with the lowest score after an agreed number of rounds, wins.

Players take on the role of an emerging opera star, gaining education and training to get jobs and roles in hopes of becoming the next opera superstar!

Education and training are gained through drafting cards from the standard deck and putting these cards in play by "purchasing" them out of your hand. Job and Role cards are won at the end of each round as players qualify for and bid on the face up Opportunity cards.

The object of the game is to be the Diva with the most stars at the end of the game. Stars are earned by collecting Role cards and drawing Special cards.

Game description from the publisher:

In those far-off days when people had already ceased to live on the trees, but did not yet know how to make cars, the main occupation of men was hunting. Neither mighty mammoth, nor nosy ground squirrel – indeed, not any animal could escape the watchful eyes of experienced hunters.

In Jabba-Dabba-Dû!, you'll become the chief hunter of a tribe and go to the hunting grounds as the head of the mighty men. Get the most thick and tasty mammoth, and bring it to your women. But beware as your tribe in not the only one on these plains – and these frail parodies of men consider themselves hunters, too! Prove that it's not true! Leave them behind in pursuit of the prey!

The players are fighting over 5 hunting locations. Each turn, you select two cards, playing them face down until they are revealed to the group. The cards are the 5 hunting locations while the 6th card is another caveman hunter, allowing you to double up at that location. The cards are revealed and you transfer your cavemen figures to the marker. Each turn there is a winner at the hunting ground based on the highest number of cavemen there. The winner gets the highest number of victory points, which varies from hunting location to hunting location. The player with the most points wins!

Game description from the publisher:

Dark Rages, a new expansion to the competitive deck-building game Nightfall, brings you a complete all-new set of cards with special powers, and features new vampires, werewolves, hunters and ghouls.

Dark Rages also introduces new Avatar cards that allow you to customize your decks' themes like never before! Will you be a Vampire Overlord or a Werewolf Alpha – and with which other groups will you ally?

description:
Under the concept "Playing with Art", Rui Alípio Monteiro came up with Covert, a game that is based on the Rock, Scissor, Paper game. The "Thread" has been added to balance things out. Although mainly for kids, this is a great game to be played by adults alike.

The idea is to make kids become familiar with geometric shapes and colours as well as doing basic maths at the same time. 
- The Scissor wins against both the Thread and the Paper.
- The String wins against both the Rock and the Paper.
- The Paper wins against the Rock
- The Rock wins against the Scissor

So, according to the power of each object, these are worth either 1 or 2 Points. Each shape appears several times, so players will be able to make calculated guesses on what would be the best piece to play at a given time. The stakes increase if both players play the same piece simultaneously, since these will accumulate to the Victory Points.

A game to be played in little time with net to no set-up time! Check out more info here:
www.criacoesasolo.com

A war hero son of the Union's brilliant president, Colonel James Curtis Edison was executing dangerous missions before his eighteenth birthday. Armed with the best weapons and technology the Union can muster, Edison is a one man army, capable of bringing down the largest of soldiers with the flick of a switch and a pull of a trigger.

Tannhäuser: Edison includes one beautifully painted figure and accompanying character sheet, an all-new scenario, and all the tokens and equipment cards necessary to bring Edison's inspiring leadership to your battles.

boardgame: Richie Rich'$ Rich Man-Poor Man Card Game
description: This hand-management card game is a rummy variant that is themed on the Richie Rich comic book character. The deck consist of 36 cards, 2 each of 18 professions, ranging from Hobo to Skywriter to Doctor. After dealing the shuffled cards to the players, the first player draws a card from the person to her right. She then lays down all matched pairs in her hand. Play continues to the left. The last player to have a card in her hand wins.

boardgame: Sergeants Miniatures Game: Hasty Positions Normandy Expansion
description:
From the publisher:

"The "Hasty Positions Normandy Expansion" brings further depth and exciting gameplay to the world of SMG."

"Each terrain piece has integrated modifiers for SHOOT, LOOK, HIDE and MOVE exactly like your terrain tiles, making it easier to defend your areas of the map. Placing your positions to hold key areas of movement or setting up interlocking fields of fire will require the use of different tactics to keep the enemy at bay. Terrain ups the level of game play by introducing new elements of strategy and planning into the game through use of 3D terrain.  New scenarios are included surrounding the new terrain involved.

This is a simple game in which players spin A spinner to collect Colorform pieces that correspond to their player mat. The game comes with two sets of Colorforms that depict characters from the Where's Waldo Now book. The Colorforms are placed randomly on the spinner at the start of the game. Each turn, a player spins the spinner and can collect a Colorform from the quadrant of the spinner that the arrow points to provided the sticker matches their player mat. The first player to collect all four characters for their mat wins.

description:
From the game publisher's web site:

"RoShamBo (aka Rock Paper Scissors) is a board game that takes an age-old concept and transforms it into a game like no other on the market — ever. Ro Sham Bo is simple yet complex — an entertaining game that makes play fast, intense, and sometimes personal, but most of all fun.

It's pretty simple (but for those of you who have been living under a rock — no pun intended…) Rock beats scissors, scissors beats paper, paper beats rock. Got it so far?

Players advance toward the winner's level by rolling the dice or by winning challenges.

However, more is at stake than just winning the game (on the board) — challenges can go beyond traditional game play.

Along the way players land on spaces that call for challenges that range from moving ahead a few spaces and changing places with the winner, to being everyone's butler for the rest of the game, or doing a crazy stunt or challenge of the cardholder's choice.

"A Rollicking Game of Skill" proclaims the box. And it must have been very skilful, perhaps too skilful.

The game includes 6 wooden egg cups which are placed in the centre of the table. The players have a catapult device with which they lob the egg shaped pieces into an egg cup. At least, that is what they intend to do but it must have been devilishly difficult. The most successful player wins, but I suspect the only player to get it right wins automatically.

Mad Science The Card Game is a family friendly game played in three phases. Players must first construct a creature from bodies and parts. then they battle the Horrible Experiment created by  Dr. Emil Knutberger to save the city. Finally, they fight each other, knocking off parts until only one creation remains standing.

Phase One is purely cards while Phases Two and Three also make use of a D6.

The core set works best with 2 or 3 players. Expansion sets allow for up to 6 players. There is also a Phase Two Solitary version of play.

description:
World-renowned genius (and eccentric) inventor Klaus von Essen has completed his latest creation, the "Apparatus". The world wants to know what it does. His peers want to know how it works. His wife wants to know what it did with him.

In Apparatus, players race to recruit Researcher teams to invent the Contraptions that will help fund the recreation of the Apparatus. Of course building the first Apparatus is important, but not as important as exploiting it for financial gain. As the research teams are recruited, each Contraption is endowed with ever-increasing grants, and a player wins only if he collects the most Electros (the currency in the game)!

In the first part of the game, players use grants that provide income every turn to recruit Researchers of various Professions. Some of these Researchers are in high demand and a bidding war between players can break out. As the player's spend their Electros on Researchers, various Contraptions that comprise The Apparatus will gain value in the second part of the game. Once all the Researchers are acquired by the players, the game moves on to the second phase.

In the second phase of the game, players use recruited Researchers to compete to be the first ones to discover the various Contraptions that will help fund the creation of the Apparatus. Each Contraption requires certain types of teams of Researchers. Players compare values of the various teams to determine the winner and second place teams. The winner has a choice of gaining Electros, which is used to determine who wins, or gaining research notes, which determines who builds the Apparatus first. As the Contraptions are decided, the required teams become more and more difficult to build.

The game ends with the building of the Apparatus, which has a large reward for the first place team. Players then count up their Electros, and the player with the most wins the game!

In this game, players are trying to move a pawn from one side of the board to the other.  Each turn a player may move a pawn one space, and can add a wall piece to the board that blocks the progress between play areas.  The winner of the game is the one that can first move his piece across the opponent's border.

The land is undiscovered...but pioneers will put roots down, and form outposts in areas with rich resources. But outposts are limited by the local resources they have available to them, and so, the railways begin to connect the outposts to trade with each other. Soon, outposts become towns, and then cities. How will your country develop?

A surprisingly simple game of country building, city connecting, and pick-up-and-delivering for one. The player places domino-like cards to create a map. Land cards indicate both natural resources and railways links. Outpost cards have both resource production abilities and resource demands. When placing outpost cards, gains resource cubes based the outpost's ability to produce a resource that matches the natural resources of neighboring land cards. If another outpost becomes linked to a another outpost via a connecte railway, and one outpost hHas a resource demand while the other has a matching production cube, then that resource can be delivered, increasing the recieving outpost to a town. A second delivery increases it to a city.

Designed for the 2012 print-and-play Solitaire Contest.

Ditsö is a strategy game of skirmish battles for all the family, that shows native Costa Rican mythology, language, customs and traditions.

The game recreates the fight among gods and other magical beings to take over Tisingal, the place where, according to the legend, lay the lost gold mines of Talamanca.

With Ditsö the player get used to the Bri Bri language and learns about the importance of some symbols of great value for our ancestors, like the fact that Sibö is the creator according to the Bri Bri, Cabécar and Brunca mythologies,or the meaning of fur or feathers for the indigenous warriors.

The name of the game itself was thinked with this purpose. Ditsö means "group" or "clan", which is represented by character cards that each player chooses to form his/her tribe. Each card has an explanation about what each character means in the native mythology, and the locations and unique figures keep their indigenous names.

In terms of gameplay, Ditsö is played like a miniatures game, althoough it uses cards (folded in the middle so they can stand) instead of miniatures. Each player or team has a certain amount of points (100 points in a standard game) to recruit an army; each card has a value. It is allowed to recruit as many green cards of the same type as whished, but only one golden card of the same kind may be recruited.

Then players set their armies in the predesignated start zones and the game begins. Depending of the charactes chosen, they may have special abilities, but normally the options will be to start a melee combat, a ranged combat or just to move (some characters have the ability of flying). There is also the chance to counter attack, but the character should have this ability to do so.

The first player of team to destroy his/her opponent wins the game.

Ditsö won the 2011 Ideas Innovadoras Award in the social innovation cathegory, and is an attempt to rescue Costa Rican roots in a fun and familiar way.

The game of Al-Jabar is based on concepts of color-mixing familiar to most of us from childhood, and on ideas from abstract algebra, a branch of higher mathematics. Once you are familiar with the rules of the game, your intuitive notions of color lead to interesting and often counter-intuitive color combinations created in gameplay.

While the mathematics involved uses elements of group theory, a foundational topic in abstract algebra, understanding the game is not difficult and requires no mathematical background. A game involves reducing your original hand of colored pieces before the other players.

Each player takes a turn by exchanging any combination of 1, 2 or 3 pieces from his or her hand for a set of 1, 2 or 3 pieces from the Center having an equal sum of colors. After the exchange, pairs of like-colored marbles are canceled from the center and replaced by a black marble, and the other players receive black marbles as well.

description: Fireball Forward is an innovative rules set for re-creating company-scale engagements in WWII using tabletop miniatures from 15mm to 28mm scale. The game is easily learned, fast-playing, and above all it emphasizes story elements of the battle so that the narrative which emerges has a natural feel of a historical written account. Attention is focused on active units, and the full story of each activation is followed until a natural break point is reached, similar to the end of a paragraph in a historical memoir. The game turn has no fire phase, rally phase, etc., but instead, active units may fire, move, rout & rally in any order as needed to develop their plan. Non-active units play an important role at all times, looking for opportunities to derail the active plan. Fireball Forward is playable on a kitchen table in one evening, and it's designed to challenge players with realistic tactical choices, resulting in a satisfying game for everyone.

Two Variants of "Stapeldammen"There are at least two different games called "Stapeldammen" (i.e. "stacking checkers"), which are played in the Netherlands. They are both boardgames of abstract strategy.

				
				
					Variant IStapeldammen I has been reported to exist at least since the early 1960s, although it may be much older.

It is played on a 10×10 checkers board with 20 pieces each. The goal is to capture or block all pieces of the opponent. The usual rules of International Checkers apply, notably backwards capture and maximum capture.

But there are three exceptions:

     Whenever a checker is captured, it is put under the checker capturing. That way stacks are built. A stack belongs to the player who owns the topmost piece, and it moves accordingly. If a stack is captured, then only the topmost piece is removed and it is put at the bottom of the stack capturing.
     There is no promotion. Pieces reaching the back rank stay there unless they can leave it by means of a backwards capture.
     A stack can be captured several times in one capturing move, provided that the capturing stack has captured other pieces in between.


Draws appear to be impossible in Stapeldammen.

Stapeldammen was an inspiration for Emergo.

Online Play


     http://www.mindsports.nl/ - (turn-based)


				
				
					Variant IIStapeldammen II was probably created by Tim ter Kuile in the late 1960s.

Rules:

Stapeldammen II is played on a checkered board of 10x10 squares. The left corner square of White must be dark. Each player (either called "White" or "Black") owns 20 men of his color, either light or dark ones. Initially these men are stacked in twos on the player's first two rows and thus forming stacks. Gameplay proceeds only on the dark squares.

Black starts. The pieces (single men and stacks alike) may move one square diagonally towards the opponent unto the adjacent square, if it is vacant, until they reach the opponent's back row, where they cannot move any longer (but still capture!). The man on top of a stack determines its ownership. It is not permitted to pass a move.

You can capture opponent's men, which are on diagonally adjacent squares by leaping over them. The piece performing the capture must land immediately beyond (similar to the short checkers leap) on a vacant square. It is allowed to jump forwards or backwards. Opponent's men that were leaped are taken immediately and are placed beneath the leaping piece so that stacks are formed or increased in height. If a stack is leaped only its upmost man is captured. Capturing must continue as long as possible and the sequence must be taken which captures most. A stack may only be leaped more than once in a turn if another man is captured in-between. Captures have priority over non-capturing moves.

The game ends when (at his turn) a player is unable to move. Each player receives as many points as he got men on the opponent's back row including opponent's men which are there under his control. Any men that are not on either player's back row are considered to be neutral and are not counted. The player who got more points wins. It is exceedingly rare that both players achieve the same number of points, which would result in a tie.

boardgame: Imps Vs Puppies: Crate of Carnage Edition
description:
This set collects the base, as well as the first two expansions to Imps Vs Puppies.

The main version of Imps Vs Puppies is a simple trick taking game in which each player sends an Imp from their team to smash a crate.  If the Imp is sent face up, they all have unique abilities which will alter the course of the round but if an Imp is sent face down, the only thing that matters is the strength. boardgame: Throwdown!: The Robot Cowboy Samurai Edition
description:
THROWDOWN! is a turn based card game that uses the Draw-Attack-Discard system or D.A.D.s. During the game you will have a character, a deck, and a discard pile. First you need at least 2 or more people to play and each person will need to have their own character card (a Robot, a Cowboy, or a Samurai), and deck, (the deck contains only 50 cards and is full of attacks, equipment, and modifiers). The objective of the game is to attack your opponents deck. You do this with your character card and cards you draw from your deck. You win when their deck is completely depleted. No matter how many people play you will always attack the person to your left, so until you get down to two players you cannot attack the person that attacked you.

Setting up to play is easy just place your character card next to your deck and your discard pile will go on the other side of your character. Flip a coin to see who goes first, then follow 3 easy steps. STEP 1 DRAW - On your turn DRAW a card from your deck and place it in front of your character card. STEP 2 ATTACK - Your character must ATTACK on your turn and will automatically defend your deck on your opponents turn. Total and resolve conflict damage. Follow any additional rules on the card. STEP 3 DISCARD - Now discard the card in play and put it into your discard pile, it is now your opponents turn and they follow the 3 easy steps.

boardgame: A Game of Thrones: The Card Game – The House of Black and White
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The House of Black and White introduces sixty new cards (three copies each of twenty different cards) that further enrich the "Beyond the Narrow Sea" cycle and its exploration of the people, places, and events of Essos. House Stark features prominently in this Chapter Pack, but players will also find a host of high-impact locations for each House, as well as an arsenal of Weapons and other attachments.

Meanwhile, The House of Black and White provides fantastic and deadly tools to players who favor the unique personalities of George R.R. Martin's A Song of Ice and Fire. The Many-Faced God may wait at the end of the road for all individuals, but two plots, a location, and an event allow players to make greater stories of the lives of those characters who stand unique in A Game of Thrones: The Card Game.
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: A Game of Thrones – The Card Game (LCG)

description:
Career Challenge® is a board game specifically designed to help students explore careers that may be good matches for their talents, interests, and work preferences. Players learn important facts about many careers and are encouraged to explore careers about which they previously had no knowledge or interest. The play of the game is a lot like a quiz show. Players answer questions to progress, and they receive checks as rewards. There are four decks of cards that the players use to learn about careers. The game comes with two sets of cards in each color. One set is designed for third and fourth graders, and the more difficult cards are designed for fifth and sixth graders. The four kinds of cards are as follows: 1. The Education and Skills cards help players learn about the importance of formal education and specific skills for various careers. 2. The What’s It Like? cards teach players about what it’s like to work in various careers. 3. The Good Career cards challenge players to match careers with interests and talents. 4. When a player picks a Guess My Career card, the player tries to get other players to guess the career by verbal descriptions and/or acting out the career.

Players also learn the importance of a college education for entering many careers, as well as beginning to think about job preferences, such as whether they want to work mainly on their own or with others. During the post-game discussion, players begin to focus on major industry groups and even individual careers.

The players race against Eric, leader of the "Wölfe von Ragnarök", to be first at the gate af Ragnarök.
On their turn a player chooses how many dice rolls he wants to perform. Than he rolls the die that many times and moves his pawn forward. If he rolls the X the players turn is over and Eric moves the number of spaces the player chose to roll his die.
The player who reaches the gate first befor Eric does wins the game. If Eric reaches the gate first, all players lose.

Alcatraz: The Scapegoat - Maximum Security, the first expansion for Kuźnia Gier's Spiel 2011 release Alcatraz: the Scapegoat, contains many new elements, including three new locations, one new character sheet (allowing for play with up to five people) and four new blackmail cards.

The expansion also introduces new mechanisms not present in the basic game:


    The Chief of Security, who can make the prisoners' life even harder than in the basic game.
    The Sucker, a character who cannot complete tasks but who in turn cannot be the scapegoat.
    Sentence cards that give your characters extra abilities.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: Target game that uses a gun made of cast-metal. The targets are Scottish soldiers in their kilts. Most successful hits wins when the game ends.

Travel the Old West of Jim Bridger, Wyatt Earp, Wild Bill Hickok, Calamity Jane, George Custer and Geronimo. Discover the answer to over 2000 questions in this fun-filled exciting adventure in Old West Historical Trivia.

The object of the game is to mover across the board on one of the three trails into the West by correctly answering the questions and arriving safely at your destination. You may choose The Oregon Trail, the Stagecoach Trail to the California gold fields or the Cattle Trail from Texas to Montana. This game can be played by individuals or teams. You may have as many on a team as you like. More than one team or individual may travel on the same trail.

Are you equipped for life in all five dimensions?

Easy 6 sided dice version for most game enthusiasts

First the military war with great battles between armies and aircraft to capture territories with victory points and sea battles between convoys, fleets and aircraft on sea zones.

Second the economic crisis, the struggle for economic growth  by building up more production on territories and the acquisistion of territories with oil production. alternatively the oil needeed for the movement of the armed forces and production can be traded for gold.

The player who increases his production values or acquires much gold receives extra victoty points.

The nations (players) start play in different alliances but the competition to build up the alliance is important to bring victory.

Third the diplomatic pressure to win new allies territories bringing more victory points either through negotiation or use of the strengths of the other dimensions, coupled with the luck of the dice.

Fourth the technological race, use of the territories production to pay for the develop more powerful armies and aircraft or more efficient production for use in both peace and war.

Fifth the moral conflict between good and evil in all the nations or even within you. Each player takes a nation which has a position on the Moral Status path. At each level there are differnt possibiliites or restrictions for their military war, dipolomacy, economic crisis and technology race.

Players may decide to move to lower levels in the search for victory.

The game can be shortned or lengthened by setting a lower or higher number of victory points neeeded to win the game. The winning alliance and the winning player is assessed.

Each dimension affects the others giving a realistic Moral Conflict experience.

The players in an alliance take their turn togther resulting in bettter co-operation and much less down time (waiting).

Reality Change
Teamwork, strategy, diplomacy, negotiation and an understanding of moral decision making are taught. The players are encouraged to practice these skills in play and in everyday life.

Armed with the Moral Conflict Experience guide book, containing historical background and practical strategy in five dimensions, players can achieve victory in both the game and in real life.

A card-laying game with verbs based on the well-known Mau-Mau game. A card can be played when it shows either the same person with another activity as on the face-up card or another person with the same activity.

The first player who can put all their cards down wins the game.

Venari Solis
Adventure on a cosmic scale

-Battle ruthless space pirates
-Discover alien worlds
-Control the galactic trade empire

Description:
25XX AD: The leader of the most power trade corporation in known space is on his death bed.

You are one of the chosen few he hand picked to be a successor if you can prove yourself.

His rules, to go into space alone, explore new sectors and find enough of the rare resources needed to build warpcores, the very linchpin of the galactic economy.

Going into space alone, in uncharted territory, against fierce rivals, and pirates, sounds crazy doesn't it? Some would say walking away from a chance for $1.5 Quintillion is crazier.


Venari Solis is a space exploration game for 2 to 4 players, it features resource collection, area control, and combat against other players and the game itself.

Play defensively, setting up mining stations in a quiet corner of space, or offensively, deploying a series of attack drones and cutting a swath of chaos across the cosmos. In the end, only one can stand victorious.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
An old style game. Players build their wealth by speculating on stock exchange, takes loan, investing in knowledge, expliot chances of life (means events).

Mechanic is standard -I go you go -.  Interaction is  little. Resources to buy are plentful and players really don't have to compete with each other.

Purpose of game is to collect the most wealth.

A game with lot of banking and 'economy' mechanics. 
A loose adaptation of Monopoly in science fiction climate of future humanity - we have similar buying system and board to Monopoly.
Edition of this project was limited.

In this game you have to make the animal sound that respresents an other player, and an animal sound to ask that player for a card. If you have 3 identical animal card you have a Tetteretet (Sound of a Dutch Elephant). 

     Make the sound of the animal your are
     Say Tetteretet
     Make the sound of the set animals 3 times and lay down the 3 cards


The game stops when a player has no cards left.
Winner is de player with the most sets

Horn Card.
When a player is asked for a card he doesn't have, he can give the horncard instead and will receive the asked card in return.

If a player neither has the card that was asked for, nor the horn card, the player makes a mouthfar. The player turn ends and the player takes an extra card from the pile. The game is played clockwise.

A very good game for young children, familycardgame, training material for speech problems. The game gets better later at night. Especially funny in an international setting since animal sound differ in each country.

Published by Sensalot

Players play characters called 'Paszcze'. They compete with each other walking on board and draw random cards of food. If they get a collection of the same food they stored it in the fridge. Besides we have in game 'Trolls'. They are horrible creatures that we may find in journey of searching food. When we find such a beast we loudly call "Troll"! Each player get it from board Troll figure (which always minus 1 to number of players) and the player which don't catch the figure lost all content of his fridge.

Family game. With lot of emotions and funny things. Horrible sort of food, like old stinking socks, rats or bucket with dishwater.

The first player which load his fridge up to maximum wins the game.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Horse lovers will enjoy the view of their favorite animal while playing the Hooray for Horses Card Game. A great game for horse lovers, the Hooray for Horses Card Game includes thick and large cards that are perfect for growing card players. The Hooray for Horses Card Game includes 48 colorful game cards with instructions to play 5 games:

1) Hooray for Horses - In this game, whoever is the first person to get rid of all his/her cards shouts "no more horses" and is the winner.

2) No More Horses! - The object of this game is to match the horses shown on the cards and to win the most pairs.  When one of the players uses up all his/her cards, or when there are no cards left in the deck, the game ends and points are calculated.

3) Color Rummy - You have to be the first player to colelct 5 cards of the same color.

4) Classic Rummy - The object is to be the first player to match all the cards in his/her hand by collecting 3 cards picturing one horse and 4 cards picturing another.

5) Horse Memory - You have to collect the most pairs.  As the game is played, players claim matches until all the cards are claimed.  Whoever has the most matching pairs is the winner.

The beautifully detailed illustrations on these playing cards depict breeds of breathtaking horses to help kids identify and name each type of horse. The Hooray for Horses Card Game is the perfect game for horse lovers and card players alike!

Dastardly Virxles have invaded the mainframe and are running amuck.  You, as a system administrator, must load your program into a detonation node and set off chain reactions, scoring points for how many you get.  The chaining system can lead to massive scores and ultimate victory.

At the beginning of the game, players select which Virxle (color) they are going after and set up an initial board.  Then, on their turn, they can play a card (Special Program or Circuit Card), load a program (placing a token on a detonation node), or run a program (setting off the program and scoring Virxles in your path).  Circuit Cards contain Virxles and detonation points, which players put down, building the board as they play. When running a program, the detonation node has a set distance (in one of the cardinal directions) the explosion travels.  If it can reach another detonation point, a chain begins and can continue for up to 9 detonations points.  Bonus points are scored for longer chains.

More advanced rules variants introduce Virxles the player must avoid as they become negative points.

Early in the game, players can freely exchange between their hand and up cards.

Player may any time lays down up to 3 cardswith equal value.
The next players may ask one or more cards of equal value or greater than those already laid.

If by chance a player can not ask for cards that meet this rule, it collects all the cards already laid down and adds those to his hand.

First player who gets rid of his cards wins the game

In essence, Lilly Frog is a 4-in-a-row game for two players. The game is played on a 6x6 checkered board. The pattern consists of green water lilly leaves and purple lotus flowers. The playing pieces (6 per player) are cutely crafted wooden frogs in the same colors.

In the initial set-up 3 frogs per player are standing on both baselines; green frogs start on the green leaves, purple frogs on the purple flowers.

A turn consists of moving a frog of the own player color to an adjacent, free space. The color of the spaces doesn't matter any more at this point. The first player to get 4 frogs in a row, wins the game.

A game variant with hopping frogs allows for a faster game.

Lilly Frog is part of the same series as ChessQuito of the same editor.

Similar to Blackjack

The goal of the game is to win as many gold pieces as possible. To win the gold pieces in play, You must reach 21 or get close but not to exceed.

The game consists of 54 cards and 10 pieces of gold. Each card has a value  2 to 10 and flag cards selectable value 1 or 11.

Each player gets 2 cards.
The goal is to reach or come as close as possible to 21, everyone will, each in turn, decide to stop or request an additional card, but must be careful not to exceed 21!
When all players have stopped, everyone turns over his cards and comparing results. The player with 21 or that the closest wins the gold coin. The player with over 21 lose one gold this round.

If 2 or more players have tied having 21 they each won a gold coin taken from the treasure, unless a player has two cards with only 21, he has a "Blackjack" and then won 2 pieces of gold.
If 2 or more player have tied having less than 21, nobody takes the gold but one more gold piece are taken into play for the next round.

The winner is the player who has won the most gold coins when the treasure is empty or there are no more cards in the deck.

An educational game designed to demonstrate how health care costs work in the U.S. health care system.

Players roll and move around a circular track, paying and receiving money as directed on the squares they land on, or on "Cost" cards drawn when they land on a "Cost" square.
Each player has a varying Salary, which determines how much money they receive when they land on Collect Your Income squares; and a varying Insurance Status (Uninsured, Underinsured or Insured), which affects how much they have to pay on "Cost" squares.

When a player runs out of money they are in "Limbo" and take no further part in the game.  The last player surviving outside "Limbo" is the winner.

A deck of cards each of which bears either three English prefixes (such as "CON- PRE- EX-"), three English suffixes, or one English root word to which prefixes and suffixes can be added.  There are also five special "prize" cards which bear one suffix each ("-ANT", "-IBLE", "-EE", "-OR" and "-FUL").

Rules for five different games to be played with the cards ("Mode I" through "Mode V") are included.

In "Mode I", the game described at greatest length, each player begins with four cards in hand, and four cards begin face up on the table.  Players on their turns must combine one card from hand with one or more face-up cards from the table, to form a word.  They may also steal a word from a previous player if they can add a card to make a new word.  If they cannot do either of those, they must instead contribute a card from their hand to the table.
Players score points for making words of four syllables or more, for making words using "prize" cards, or for using the last card from the table.

Charles Pierre Goldey of New York, New York filed an application for a U.S. patent for this card game on 16 June 1881, which was granted on 26 July 1881: US244745A.

Tit 3 Tat is game #30 of the Games on Half a Checkerboard Series.

Tit 3 Tat is a 3 player only game on half a checkerboard, with each player having 6 pieces of their color.  In the game, players attempt to control more spaces on the board by the time the game ends.  Pieces move like chess queens, and have the ability to pass through spaces control by their side.  Each turn, a player must either move one of their pieces on top of a single piece of an opponent, or remove an enemy piece on top of a stack from two players (this is in the basic version, with a variant providing more complex removal rules). Effort was made to provide a 3 player game experience that would try to prevent players from colluding to gang up against a single player in order to eliminate them from play.

The game provides a unique feature where it is possible for a player to win a game, even if they haven't made a move.  In addition, turn order is not fixed, and depends totally on who a player interacts with during their turn.

Heute schon geküsst? is a co-operative game for 2 players (one couple).
Players alternately draw cards. This card either show questions or tasks.

If it is a question card, one player secretly writes down the answer and the other one has to guess the right answer. The questions are about personal wishes, preferences, ideas, etc., for example "How many children do you want?"
If the answer is correct, the couple sores 3 points (move the pink token), otherwies the game scores 3 points (black heard moves).

If it is a task card, the player has to guess how good he can do this task, for example "Massage your partners neck", the player has to guess secretly who good he can do this. (good or verry good). Than he does the task and the other player rates the efforts. If the rating fits the guess, they score points (1 if it was good, 2 if it was verry good), otherwies the black heard moves.

The game ends as soon as the pink token has reached the final space on the score track. The couples success is determinated by the position of the black heard.
Undead Horrors is a Kickstarter Exclusive mini-expansion that adds a total of 7 cards. The most noteworthy addition is the Graveyard Stronghold which protects any Undead Creatures.
Dark Star is a mini-expansion that adds a total of 30 cards. It introduces two new card types: Villain and War Machine. It also provides the first Stronghold for the protection of Villains.

Yaaar!

Oh no!  You and your friends have all become shipwrecked on the dangerous Pirate Island.  Now you all have to work together to escape.

But watch out!  The island is teeming with hazards, from the treacherous Tall Peak to the deadly Raging River.  Enemies abound on Pirate Island: Headhunters, Skeletons, and - of course - Pirates.

But there is hope.  There is a rumor of a treasure buried on Pirate Island.  If you and your friends can navigate through the hazards and avoid or kill the enemies, then maybe you can follow the treasure map and find the treasure.   Then you could sneak aboard the docked pirate ship and Escape From Pirate Island!

Pirate Island is a cooperative game of exploration and risk management.  Your characters move around Pirate Island uncovering new hexes which could be helpful or hazardous.  You need to manage your food and resources, running out of either can be dangerous, and you need to fight or escape from deadly enemies.

You also need to find the hexes that contain clues to the treasure map, and then, once you have the treasure, you need to get to the lagoon to board the pirate ship to escape.  And you need to do it before time -- represented by the event deck -- runs out.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Who Took the Apple? is a cardgame played backwards in time.

The goal of the game is to take an apple which is placed under a bucket. Players "pre program" their actions, like in Space Alert or Roborally, but cards are played backwards in time: First card played will be last action happening.

In the second phase of the game, all players go through their actions to resolve what actually happened. Some actions might be impossible to carry out which creates a paradox. These cards are ignored in order to restore space/time logic. Players have to maintain multiple possible game states in their head in order to successfully play.

Three years in the making, FUBAR: Assault on Penzar Island is an amalgamation of strategy game fused with contemporary popular cultural myths and a whole lot of chaos thrown into the mix.  Up to six different factions face off to control the last remaining land mass on Earth in the very distant (or not so distant) future.

The scenario and subsequent conflict begins with an alien abduction through the fabric of time and space which is unexpectedly thwarted by a glitch, landing the warring factions on a remote island in the middle of nowhere.  The factions begin in their respective base hub on the outskirts of the island.  Quickly, they must build their ranks and establish their perimeters to begin the conquest of their adversaries. Their goal is to acquire the Mojos of their opponents and secure more territories on the island.  Factions not only have their own set of specific cards to aid them in their conquest, they also have a random faction force that enables them to have a unique edge over their foes.   To complicate matters and add an element familiar to any survivalist, there is a good chance that chaos will enter the fray.  From radiation leaks, mad cow outbreaks, and teleportation malfunctions to volcano eruptions or the periodic tsunami, the unpredictable chaos events (a dreaded favorite) will force players to adjust their strategies as the game progresses.

The victory goes to the first player that successfully absconds with an enemy’s Mojo and safely returns it to their own hub…. but the game could go on as there are chaos events that could shift the game objective at any time.  In addition, there are 4 other game variations with different objectives that players can choose from to make for a variety of gaming experiences with the same set.  There is a planned expansion/variation called Covert Ops being developed as well.

Up to four princesses try to reach the Magic Castle first to open the big ball. The active player waves the (electronic) magic wand which then gives instructions about movement (mostly letting the princess move forward, but sometimes doing spells which change the path to the castle, let a princess swap places with another princess, etc. Some fields protect the princess from spells, other yield a diamond that can neutralize spells. The first princess to reach the castle is the winner.

Footchess is a board game that simulates a soccer match. In order to win it you will need vision, strategy and some luck.

10 players divided in two teams move around a chess-like board, looking for open passing lines and a chance to score goals.

Each player starts is turn by rolling the dice in order to know is action points. Then he can spend his actions points passing the ball, moving players, intercepting the ball from the opponent or shooting the ball on goal. When the player uses all his action points the turn is over and the player picks a card from the deck. Some cards improve player skills, others are not so good and players can even be injured or kicked out of the game.
The game ends when all 40 cards have been picked.
In case of a draw the game continues until one of the teams score the winning goal.

boardgame: The Glory that Was Vol. I: Greece. Episode IV – Socrates' Battles
description:
Fourth entry in the Glory that was Greece series, is a print and play game dedicated to two battles from the Peloponnesian War, Delium 424 BC and Amphipolis 422 BC. The famous greek philosopher Socrates took part in both of these campaigns, and fought with valor.
The game uses a medium complexity system, with specific rules for the various kind of arms (hoplites, peltasts, cavalry, etc.) and their interaction. There are two integrated combat resolution charts, rules for leaders and their influence on the field of battle (both as aid in combat than in morale checks) and also optional rules with the use of combat cards for getting more variable in the combat phase.
family: Series: The Glory That Was (TCS Games)

description:
In Intelligent Design each player attempts to create the conditions necessary for life to evolve in his/her solar system.  The players do this by flicking planets across the board and utilizing gravitational fields to move the planets around each other and the suns.  Players must also be wary of the black-hole in the center of the board that can swallow up the planets left within its gravity field.  The game relies upon a mix of skill and strategy creating exciting shots and chin scratching moments.

Intelligent Design is played on a board with a hexagonal grid.  The grid has 6 solar systems centered on each sun and a black-hole in the center space.  Each player has a set of 10 planets and 2 score tokens in a color that matches the color of a solar system and grid edge.  The planets each have a number between 1 and 8 printed on them and come in three different sizes.  The number on the planet indicates the mass of the planet and its ability to orbit(moving adjacent) planets with smaller numbers.  Each player will take turns flicking and orbiting planets on the board attempting to get as many planets into the Goldilocks Zone of their sun.  The planets are worth an amount of victory points based upon their size with the smallest(and most vulnerable) planets worth the most and the largest worth the least.  Players will have the opportunity to score the planets in the Goldilocks Zone of their sun three times during the course of the game, twice by using score tokens on their turn and again during final scoring.  The winner is the person that has earned the most VP throughout the game.

boardgame: Kabinettskrieg: The War of the Austrian Succession
description:
Kabinettskrieg is a Grand Campaign module combining the games Cockpit of Europe, Sport of Kings, and Queens' Gambit in one very large scenario. IMPORTANT. All three base games are required for this module.

Kabinettskrieg is a multi-player operational study, for 2-6, or even more would-be generals; each player controls the forces of one of the major participating nations and its minor allies. Extra players can lead the troops of the same nation in individual theatres of the war. Although the players are divided into two Camps, victory is an individual goal as you try to win more glory than both your allies and your enemies. Do YOU have what it takes to become the greatest captain general of the Eighteenth Century?

Included in the module is a set of the latest basic rules, charts, and tables (version 3.75) for the Lace Wars system. A few key concepts in this system include Operations Points, Campaign Plans, Prestige, and Auxiliaries. Operations Points are accumulated during periods of inactivity, in order to be spent during the course of active campaigning. If a player is unable or unwilling to spend OPs, his forces suffer attrition instead. The further forces are from their base of operations, the more severe the penalties will be. Whoever has the most OPs also has the initiative.

Map scale is 8.5 miles per hex. The counters are battalions and regiments. Units are rated for Strength (in battalions), Effectiveness (a combination of morale and training), and Movement. Turns are equivalent to months. Each turn is broken down into several phases - supply, operations, admin, etc.

The heart of the game is the Operations Phase, where the players alternate moving their formations, laying siege to fortresses and engaging in battle with the enemy field forces. The players have a degree of flexibility in what they do with their forces, but they are constrained by the Campaign Plan or plans that they choose. These dictate what objectives (usually fortresses) must be taken. A successfully completed plan will garner Prestige for a player. At the end of the game, the player with the highest prestige wins. In addition, bonuses can be won for victory in battle, and these may be used to buy rewards that improve a player's chances, or be used as influence.

Leaders have an important role to play, as befitting an era where personal command was critical. They are rated for skill or effectiveness, personality, and influence (i.e. the chance they have of retaining command despite their incompetence).

In addition to the concepts above, the supply system has been streamlined while keeping to the basic elements of foraging in tandem with the use of pre-positioned depots. River and canal movement has been taken into account and will prove as critical to success as the use of rail lines in more modern games.

The combat system has a tactical feel - while not a full sub-system with battlefield maps, it addresses the key issues of frontage, reserves, and supports, as well as firepower and morale. Winning a battle will bring you the acclaim of your noble peers, but may not gain you any strategic advantage; losing a battle can be catastrophic.
