description:
It's a game similar to chess and checkers, the background is the first world war, german versus french. 
Each side has four different units: infantry (one move to front or side) , cavalry (one diagonal move, like checkers game), cannons (like towers in chess) and planes/zeppelins (like queens in chess). 
There is a special unit, the standar-bearer, if you capture the enemy standar-bearer with one infantry or cavalry unit, your unit change to a cannon unit.
The board is divided by a river in the middle, the infantry and cavalry units cannot retreat until overpass the river. 
The objetive to win is eliminate all the enemy units.

Fantail Line presents "Fantail Baseball"
Fantail Baseball is a board game agile and very fun where two teams try to get more runs that your opponent and so win the match.
Smart Charts printed on both sides, (4) per team, are designed so that the rules and tactics of baseball can be developed on the game board.
The symbols printed on the game board are directly related to the symbols printed on smart charts.
A twelve-sided roll dice is used by both teams in turn.
Each smart chart has a space at the top which takes into account the throwing of the pitcher, being the possibilities strike, ball and ball in play.
The different options related to the steal of bases are represented in smart charts.
The team that is in the field, throw the 12-sided roll dice by means of your pitcher to determine if the throwing becomes a strike, a ball or a hit the ball into the field.
The team that is at bat, throw the same 12-sided roll dice to determine whether steals bases, if makes a full strike, if produces a home run or if the runner is out.
The smart chart in use will indicate the different positions of the pieces of both teams on the board and also of the ball.

boardgame: Shenandoah: A Civil War Miniatures Campaign System
description:
A set of campaign rules with a map for playing a campaign game set in 1863 in the Shenandoah Valley.

Comes with a set of counters, 2 grand tactical maps, and the rules booklet

Swedish trick-taking game from at least early 19th century. Players are dealt five cards. Basic trick-taking rules: follow suit, go over if possible, winner of the trick leads to the next. Winner of the last trick wins the hand.

Adults used to play the game for money, nowadays the game is mostly used to teach trick-taking basics to children.

The hit card game "Poker" but in dice form all in a self contained plastic tray with tumblers to contain and roll the dice.

Players will be trying to get the highest scoring combination of dice, the best of which wins the round and gets points associated with their combo of dice. Rounds start off by rolling the dice up to three time during their turn. their first roll has to be done with all five dice but the second and third roll they must leave at least one but up to four dice unchanged. Players can score after their first or second roll but after the third roll they must score their dice. The ranking order of hands are the same as conventional poker. You win by having the most points at the end of a predetermined set of rounds.

DICEcapades! Quick Play Edition is a smaller version of DICEcapades that can be either played in half the time as a standalone game or used as an expansion for the original game. At the start of a player's turn, he draws a card from one of the decks, then tries out perform the challenge on the card, possibly with the assistance of others. The three categories of cards are:


    Actionland cards are generally physical or dexterity challenges, such as drawing a picture, stacking the dice before time runs out, or doing 1d20 jumping jacks.
    Thinkingtown cards are generally memory, logic, or math exercises, such as naming a certain number of items in a category, gaining/losing a point if the product of the numbers of the dice is even or odd, or rolling letter dice to spell a person's name within thirty seconds.
    Triviaville cards present trivia questions, with an opponent rolling a color die, then asking the corresponding question. (Black = reader's choice.)


The player(s) performing the challenge score one or more points (if they succeed), and the first player to score twenty points wins. (The rules suggest playing in teams, so the maximum player count could be considered twelve.)

boardgame: Flames of War: Firestorm – Greece: The Italian and German Invasions of Greece
description:
The Battle of Greece 1940-1941 using the established Firestorm System for Flames of War. The Firestorm system is designed to be used with the Flames of War but the Game can be played as a solo game under the General's Game rules.

Firestorm Greece recreates the Battle for Greece in four turns, each with a number of battles.

TURN 1 - Emergenza G - The Italian invasion on the 28 October 1940
TURN 2 - The Greek Counter-Offensive, November to January. Saw the Greeks push into Albania
TURN 3 - Primavera - Italian Spring Offensive under the personal command of Mussolini
TURN 4 - Unternehmen Marita, the German Invasion of Greece in April.

The first three turns see the struggle of the Greek and the Italian forces on the Greek Albanian boarder while Unternehmen brings the German Blitzkrieg to Greece while the British Expeditionary force W-Force under Lt. General Sir Henry Maitland Wilson adds a little to the Greek defence.

This game is available as a free download from the Flames of War website.

boardgame: Adventure 7: The Rescue of Princess Millandriell (fan expansion for HeroQuest)
description:
This adventure has a very simple story line. So to make it more interesting we started the Heroes from beginning stats and built them up as the story moved along. The Oger and Giant Wolf really added a lot of excitment to the game so they were used a lot. We hope that you will have as much fun with this adventure as we did creating it.
The pages for this adventure are available to download at "Dragonkingdoms.co.uk" and yahoo's group site "HeroQuest-Adventures" for free. You do need to join the group to access the file's section where they are stored. The photo section of this site will allow you to read the story and look at the maps to see if this game is something you would like to play. Your adventure awaits!

A party game where players use combinations of cards to create headlines along certain topics as demanded by The Editor. Each round sees a new Editor ask for a headline based on a certain subject which can be decided upon either at random or by flipping one of the included Topic cards. By using Actions, Descriptions and Things cards, players submit a headline to the editor and hope it will catch their interest. The Editor then chooses two headlines and asks the players to summarise the stories behind them. The Editor then chooses their favourite of the two and the headline is added to a Running Order.

The Running Order acts as a scoreboard of sorts. Players will recieve points for each card they use in their headlines but also get bonuses for the positions the stories end up in at the end of the game. Every time a new headline is added to the Running Order, the Editor for that turn decides where it will be placed, bumping all other stories lower than it further down the order. If there are more than ten stories, the lowest ranked one is removed from the game scoring that player no points. At the end of the game, the lead story recieves a bonus 10 points, the second 9, the third 8, etc., all the way down to 1.

boardgame: Adventure 8: The Horror Inside the Ancient Halls of Sunca (fan expansion for HeroQuest)
description:
This Adventure takes place right after the end of The Frozen Horror, so we let the Heroes keep all that they had collected from the expansion games. My Heroes really liked having the extra mercenaries, so we made them a permanent part of the team. This changed things. As Zargon I used more monsters with stronger powers, The Yeti and War Bear are great monsters. This Adventure moves along quickly and I'm sure that you are going to have a lot of fun playing it.
The pages for this adventure are available to download at "Dragonkingdoms.co.uk" and yahoo's group site "HeroQuest-Adventures" for free. You do need to join the group to access the file's section where they are stored. The photo section of this site will allow you to read the story and look at the maps to see if this game is something you would like to play. Your adventure awaits!

Game description from the publisher:

In The Ladies of Troyes, the women make an appearance in the form of three new Character cards that you can include in your games. A new action allows players to move their guard along the ramparts in order to access one of 16 new activities outside the city. Each player is represented by a new purple die (representing the head of the family), which cannot be bought by other players. The head of the family can carry out civil, military, or religious actions — your choice!

The Ladies of Troyes also includes twenty-seven new Activity cards and six Event cards to add still more variety to the game!

On February 3, 1852 was the final war between the Argentine Confederation and the Empire of Brazil, which began in 1851 during the Uruguay civil war, in the great Battle of Monte Caseros!

Over 60,000 soldiers fought in one of the bloodiest battles in the history of South America... After this battle, for the first time in history, a non-European army marched on the streets of Buenos Aires...

Monte Caseros is a card game for two players whose theme is the Battle of Monte Caseros. Each player must manage their armies composed of Infantry, Cavalry and Artillery. Each player must correctly apply their strategic cards, trying to trap the enemy officers and annihilate your troops!

At each turn the player must place on the battlefield cards that represent their armies and, when appropriate, to attack the enemy army, applying their strategic cards. For the resolution of the fighting we use the military force of each cards, the strategic factor, and a combat resolution table as the best war games.

“A Cartomante” é um jogo voltado especialmente para o público juvenil (a partir dos 14 anos), entretanto passível de ser apreciado por outras faixas etárias por se tratar de um produto que pode ser interpretado em diversas camadas. Suas ilustrações e sua identidade visual remetem ao período que ficou conhecido como belle époque dado o fato de que o conto escrito por Machado de Assis está ambientado no final do século XIX. Os cards de maior destaque no jogo estão diretamente ligados aos arcanos maiores do Tarot de Marsella, o mais tradicional oráculo de divinação trazido para os tempos atuais e também presente no conto em questão. As figuras e personagens do conto estão representadas arquetipicamente pelo significado destes arcanos nas imagens ilustradas.

Nesse jogo você tem que proteger os seus Nobres dos ataques adversários. Para isso você tem Barreiras, Artefatos, Aliados e outros tipos de cartas.

No final o jogador que tiver mais Nobres em sua área é o vencedor.


"The Fortune Teller" is a game geared especially for teenagers (from age 14 years), however that can be enjoyed by other age groups because it is a product that can be interpreted in several layers. His illustrations and visual identity refer to the period that became known as the "belle epoque" given the fact that the short story by Machado de Assis is set in the late XIX century. The most prominent cards in the game are directly linked to the major arcana of Marseille's Tarot, the most traditional oracle of divination brought to the current times and also present the story in question. The figures in the story and characters are represented by the meaning of these archetypal images illustrated in arcane.

In these game you must protect your Nobles against the other players cards. To do that you can use Barriers, Artifacts, Allies and ohter types of cards.

At the end, the player with most Nobles at your playing area is the winner.

ASH Poker, which gets its name from being a fusion of Stud and Hold'em Poker.  It is a game in the poker family, which adds auctioning off draw cards so players may fill and improve their hands.  It borrows the dealer button, and large and small blinds from Texas Hold'em and also each player having their own set of cards from Stud Poker.

In the game, after the big and small blinds are set, and cards are dealt, there is a round of betting.  After this, draw cards are auctioned off, with money paid to obtain draw cards put in the pot.  After this, another round of betting occurs.  High hand takes the pot, or high hands split the pot.

The game has a number of variants that vary the number of draw cards and rounds of draw cards per hand, and also determine whether or not there are limits on bids and bets.

With King of Tokyo: Power Up! – an expansion for the King of Tokyo base game – Included is the new monster Pandakai, a giant panda bear. After choosing a monster, each player takes the eight Evolution cards associated with that monster, shuffles those cards, and creates a personal deck. At the end of a player's turn, if she has three hearts, she draws one Evolution card and adds it to her hand; this is possible even in Tokyo (when hearts are normally useless) and even if the hearts are used for some other action, such as healing.

A player can reveal and play an Evolution card at any time. Some Evolutions are temporary, allowing a one-time bonus, while others are Permanent, such as Gigazaur's "Tail Sweep", which allows him to change one die to a 1 or 2 each turn. Each Evolution card also identifies whether a monster is a Mutant, Invader, or Robot, and while not relevant for Power Up!, this species identification could come into play in future expansions.

The rules for King of Tokyo: Power Up! contain a few variants: Players start with a random Evolution in play, or draw two cards and choose the one they want, or draft a set of Evolutions prior to the start of play. However you play, the goal of the game remains the same: Score 20 points or be the last monster standing amid the rubble of what was once Tokyo.

Part of the King of Tokyo series.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Do Move Say is a party game for 10 - 40 players (more if one combines decks). The game takes place standing up in a moderately open space, enough for the players to move around freely.

Do Move Say has two main layers: The Mastermind and Detective game and the Townspeople game.

The Mastermind is trying to accomplish three tasks: Disable three players, find a secret key, and pass a secret note. The Detective must stand outside of the space and determine which player is the Mastermind before the tasks are complete.

The Mastermind must complete the tasks in the midst of the Townspeople game. Each Townsperson has instructions on how to behave in the space, including two "if / then" statements and two global statements. Their behavior makes it difficult for the Detective to determine which player is the Mastermind, while also making the Mastermind's tasks challenging.

Mod cards are also available in the deck, which give one or more of the townspeople goals other than satisfying both "if / then" statements. Mods include the Doctor, who can revive disabled players, the Mimic, who copies everything he sees, and the Pamphleteer, who must distribute three notes without being detected.

The game ends once the Mastermind has completed all three tasks, the Detective has made a guess, or when time expires.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
A math domino game for 1-4 players.

At the beginning of a 2-4 player game, each player is dealt six hexagonal crab dominoes. Each crab holds a number between its claws, which must be matched to the equation of another crab. Each crab has a single equation on one of its three hind edges, in which one of the factors is replaced with an empty box, that must be filled with an answer number from another crab. For example, a crab holding the number, 4, could be attached to a crab with the equation, 8x[]=32, on its back.

Once the players have placed their six starting dominoes face up in front of them, the top domino of the draw pile is then placed in the center of the play area, and play begins.

On a player's turn, they first draw a new domino, placing it with the others face up in front of them, and then try to add as many of their dominoes as they can to the line of crabs. Players may also move crabs from the front of the line to the back of the line, and vice-versa, but only if they can immediately add a domino from their hand, to one of the two new positions. If a player can no longer add more dominoes to the line, their turn is over. If a player plays their last domino, they win the game.

Players may use the answer wheel to check their math at any time.

The game also comes with two solitaire variants.

From the publisher:

ZombieSmarts is the infectious question-and-answer game that features grisly facts about the histories, habits, and hungers of the living dead. Choose a card and learn how to combat a zombie invasion: What should you do if a zombie bites one of your friends? Can table salts reverse the effects of "zombification"? What is smarter—a zombie or a dog? True/False and multiple-choice cards offer macabre information on zombie films, Voodoo, re-killing, undead encounters, and many other gruesome topics.

From the manufacturer:

What would happen if you were bitten by a mysterious Count? How might you repel a particularly malevolent vampire? Play alone with a clove of garlic by your side, or invite your friends for a competitive vampire-athon. VampireSmarts has the answers. Inside the mesmerizing box you’ll find a VampireTips Guide, a score pad, and 100 question and answer cards in four delicious categories.

An action game based on the impending Spanish-American war.

Players shot wooden bullets at die-cut soldiers using hand-held spring-loaded cannons.

The last of four wargames introduced in 1898 by Parker Brothers themed around the Spanish-American War.

All four games were action based and involved the players shooting wooden bullets at die-cut soldiers using hand-held spring-loaded cannons.

Although not a commercial success, the series of four games contributed greatly to Parker Brothers image as a patriotic American company.

The first three games in the series were The War in Cuba, The Siege of Havana and The Battle of Manila.

Introductory game for the OCMS system from Game Production Workshop.  The current set is covering WW II in the European Theater of operations.

This game covers the campaign in Sicily using the rules, and components for the entire game series.  Set with a scale of 15 day turns, and 17 miles to the hex, it is an operational level examination of the conduct of warfare, involving ground, air, and naval forces.

The players are the commanders of the Axis and Allied forces involved in the campaign, with the historical missions assigned to both sides.

Statler made a version of "Geographical Lotto" with USA map cards and cities. Playthings Limited made essentially the same game for Canadian cards and cities. It is not known if Playthings and Statler were somehow connected.

The game is basically a bingo game whereby the player have cards which they are trying to fill. The call are made by blindly drawing a city card which is then called out. Each card has twelve cities. The winner is the player who fills his card first.

Contents:

     Geographic cards for each player with 15 cities
     City cards which to draw & call

The excitement is building with the Crazy Tower! Use the 10 bamboo discs, 19 bamboo pillars and 2 balance figures to stack your tower higher and higher, but be careful, or it all might come crashing down! It takes nerves of steel and a steady hand to make crazy towers.

In his turn player places a game-piece to tower and be careful not to collapse the tower and score minus points.

The player with the most points wins the game.

In the typical style of this publisher's Action XX games, this is not a simulation but more an abstract game. You must move your soldiers to win control of squares on the back row of the board. Meanwhile, he is doing the same to get at your back row squares. First to do so, wins. Movement is by dice so it is not an abstract game although it feels like one.

Lots of nice plastic American Civil War soldiers.

It is midnight and the ghosts can hardly wait to go to the graveyard for a night of fun but they have to be home on time. Players take turns moving around the board with the help of a rat, bat and spider. Flip the play pieces into the colored bamboo rings and move to the next colored tile of the same color. 
Players who reach back in time win. Players who don't lose.

Contents: 

     1 game board, 
     3 animals, 
     4 ghost figures, 
     1 die, 
     1 springboard, 
     7 bamboo rings and 
     one felt mat.

Statistical cricket game. Teams are broadly rated, with dice rolls and charts deciding the outcome of matches. The designer thinks that each game will take around 10 minutes after you have learned the mechanisms of play. Thus, an entire tournament can easily be played in one evening.

Statistical baseball simulation, different to the company's Brief 'n' Brisk Major League Baseball. Unusually for this owner/designer, Patrick Premo, he has co-designed this game, not published it as one of his own designs.

As usual, dice and charts combine to give match results. Results are quickly established so a whole season is a realistic possibility to complete in only a few evenings.

An amazing number of teams are rated, from 1871 to 1990.

Statistical basketball simulation. Game designed with the intention that each match-up is quickly resolved (using charts and dice as usual in these types of game). The designer hoped that this brevity would allow a player to replay a whole season in a relatively short space of time.

Statistics included for the 1991/92 season plus two older seasons. Every main player in each team is rated as well as the team ratings.

The board represents a corner of French with different routes: the path of Business, the sentimental route etc. The goal is to reach the Fortunate Isles across the entire plateau by road you choose. Different modes of transportation are available: car, rail, plane, boat.

Benefit cards are auctioned at the beginning of the game: transport, post office, restaurants, casinos, websites (one card per player). The auction amount is paid to the Fund for Tourism. The transfer takes place through the die roll for the car and paying for his trip by train or plane.

The round ends when one player reaches the lighthouse square, it gets the money up in the Bank of Tourism and each opponent receives 25 FF. Whoever wins two rounds (or one that remains after eliminating competitors by bankruptcy) wins.

"Great wildlife" in English

There are 2 questions in each card with 3 answers for each questions. The winner is whoever can answer most questions correctly and thus can collect the most cards.
If the current player answers incorrectly the next player may risk with one of his collected card to answer it.

Contents:

    32 large format cards with color photos

"Animal tracks" in English

There are 2 questions in each card with 3 answers for each questions. The winner is whoever can answer most questions correctly and thus can collect the most cards.
If the current player answers incorrectly the next player may risk with one of his collected card to answer it.

Contents:

     32 large format cards with color photos

Statistical sports game, featuring every World Cup team from 1930 to 1990. It uses the designer's innovative design to decide each match-up in double-quick time. This enables a player to play a whole tournament in one evening.

Dice rolls and charts combine to give each result.

Abstract card game where you collect sets of three or more cards.  Each card contains one to six abstract shapes, such as a green circle or a blue triangle.  A set is called a "zero sum" if it contains an even number (including 0) of every shape.

In the Seven Card Challenge, seven cards are dealt face up.  The first player to find a zero sum takes those cards, which are replaced with new cards.  When the deck runs out, the player with the most cards wins.

From the manufacturer:

Just how much of a Foodie are you? Test your skill and knowledge with this fun and educational game, Gourmet Smarts. Complete with 100 questions, a guide and a scorepad, this is a great item for anyone that's interested in cooking, entertaining or just having fun. Comes in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

Prove to your friends and family you know more about babies than they do with this fun and informative question and answer game, BabySmarts. Includes 60 questions, a BabySmarts Guide and a score pad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

A fun and informative adult game designed to teach as you play. Includes 100 questions with answers, a 12 pages tip guide and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box. Designed by experts for everyone.

From the manufacturer:

A great way to learn all there is to know about man's best friend. Contains 100 questions with answers and designed to teach as you play, it comes with a 12 pages tip guide and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

A great way to learn all there is to know about one of life's guilty pleasures, Red Wine. Includes 100 questions and answes and designed to teach as you play. Comes with a 12 pages tip guide and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

What's in a Cosmo? What about a Gimlet or a White Lady? CocktailSmarts has the answers, diving into different alcohols, history, lingo, and other intoxicating facts so you can feel as much like a pro in front of the bar as the guy or gal behind the bar. Play CocktailSmarts, enjoy the festive coasters enclosed, and add a twist to your next cocktail party.

A fabulous way to learn about a guilty pleasure and have fun doing it. Cocktail Smarts has 60 questions and comes with a set of coasters, a cocktail guide and scorepad in a sturdy and full colored tin.

From the manufacturer:

Learn everything you need to know about the city that never sleeps in this question and answer game, NYC Smarts. Filled with tons of insider information and guarateed to teach while you play, it's a great item for the resident as well as the tourist. Includes 100 questions, a Guide and a Scorepad in a sturdy flip top box measuring.

From the manufacturer:

Impress your friends and family with this fun and informative question and answer game about one of our favorite indulgences, Chocolate. Includes 60 questions, a ChocolateSmarts Guide and a score pad in a sturdy flip top box.

From cocoa tree to chocolate bars, ChocolateSmarts offers everything you ever wanted to know about this delightful treat and then some. Pick a card and explore cocao history and tips, chocolate trivia, and more. Do you know which cocoa beans are of the highest quality? The best way to melt chocolate? Or who made the first chocolate bar? You will after indulging in a box of ChocolateSmarts.

From the manufacturer:

Think you know everything there is to know about Golf? Prove it while playing this fun and informative question and answer game, GolfSmarts. Includes 60 questions, a GolfSmarts Guide and a score pad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

How does your favorite cappuccino, espresso, or cup of joe get so delicious? Find out with CoffeeSmarts, the caffeinated question and answer game filled with answers for coffee lovers - from those who just crave their morning cup to expert baristas. This stylish box og question and answer cards reveals the history and mystery behind one of the world's most popular beverages. Sit down with a steaming cup of coffee, pick a card, and discover: What's the highest quality coffee bean? What's French roast? How can you make Cowboy coffee? And what's in Spanish Coffee?

Stay up all night with your pals sipping a cup o Joe and playing this this fun and informative question and answer game about the drink we can't live without, Coffee. Includes 60 questions, a CoffeeSmarts Guide and a score pad in a sturdy flip top box.
Statistical Ice Hockey game. Uses dice rolls compared to charts to make decisions and outcomes of strategy. Includes ratings for 1990/91 NHL teams and the 1991/92 Playoffs.

Statistical replay game, designed to generate quick results, thus enabling a whole tournament to be played in an evening. In fact, in a week or so, a whole season could be replayed.

Dice rolls checked against charts determines successes or failures for the players, each player being rated in various skills.

The statistics are compiled for players on the U.S. Tour rather than the European one.

Fire up the cannons! Six enemy fleets are all competing to reign the high seas, and it’s up to you to maneuver your ships and cannons to sink as many key targets as possible. Will you be able to carry your fleet to victory?

The board consists of a grid of ship and submarine tokens, along with a command ship and cannon belonging to each player. In each round, players schedule actions on their player dashboards according to the amount of time required to carry them out. Then, each time slot is resolved simultaneously — actions include loading powder and cannonballs, maneuvering ships and cannons, and commanding the fleet of ships of a player's own color.

When a ship or submarine is hit by the appropriate size of cannonball, the token is retrieved. If a player hits another player's command ship, the player must give up a token to the player who fired the shot. The player with the most points at the end of the game is declared the winner.

Become a great commander from history and shape destiny!
Taking an army of model soldiers, you can recreate a famous battle, or enjoy a relaxing game with friends across the tabletop.

War and Conquest is an exciting and dynamic tabletop miniatures battle game.  Each player commands an army of soldiers through movement and combat phases.  The rules cover movement, shooting, charges and combats, morale, and more.  Each player fields an army constructed from free army lists utilizing a points system to arrive at historical and competitive army compositions.  Players collect and paint historical armies based on the free army lists. Combat is resolved by a single dice roll for each figure, creating a fast and streamlined system that simultaneously does not compromise on detail.

War and Conquest features randomized initiative rolls for more dynamic battles and introduces a mechanic called "Strategy Intervention Points" which allow the player to influence morale, initiative, and even terrain placement to turn the tide of battle in their favor.

boardgame: Fallen City of Karez: Golden Dragon expansion
description:
The Golden Dragon Guild is one of the oldest guild houses in the known world, and its mysterious demeanor and deity fed the rumors tying them to the destruction of Karez. As the king leaves Karez to its ruins, the Golden Dragon's worshipers can finally construct their secret temple and resurrect their beloved deity.

Fallen City of Karez: Golden Dragon  comes with a new deck of allegiance cards. The player who draws the Golden Dragon Allegiance will conspire to destroy the city of Karez! If Karez does not become a City by the end of the game, this player wins the game and all the other players lose.

Objectif Varsovie 1944 (Target Warsaw 1944) covers the second part of the soviet offensive against the Heeresgruppe Mitte. The victorious red army runs towards oriental Prussia and the polish capital city.
It expands the first game, Bagration 1944, which covered the 2 first weeks of battle and the capture of Minsk. The 2 games can either be played separately or linked as a campaign game.

Objectif Varsovie 1944 is a 2-player game, one representing the commander of German forces and the other the commander of Soviet forces. It is played on a map covering the operation theater. It is divided into hexagons representing 20 km. It uses die-cut counters representing military units that participated in this campaign: German divisions and Russian army corps. A full game lasts 15 turns, each representing two days of real time.

Ziplock game with:
- one A2-size map
- 192 die-cut counters
- 16 pages full color rules (both French and English)
- A quick reference aid
- A german display for operation points
- A soviet display for operation points

Two friends are riding their horses to a tree and back, trying to collect berries on the way. Essentially, this is a roll and move game for young kids. However, the player first place a hidden bet on the winner, and they can move either of the two riders as far as their die roll commands. When a rider reaches the farm again, anyone who had placed the right bet receives points; additional points are gained through picking up berries. The player with most points is the winner.

From the manufacturer:

Splain that if you can! The nostalgic question and answer game that makes learning about the most beloved TV series of all time easy and fun.

Think you know everything there is to know about I Love Lucy? Well prove it and have a great time doing it with this 100 question and answer game that's designed to teach as you play. Comes with a 12 pages tip booklet and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

A fabulously fun way to learn about the King, Elvis Presley. Written by experts and designed to teach, this question and answer game covers 4 topics, Legend and Legacy, Music and Magic, On the Silver Screen and On The Road. Also comes with a 12 pages Elvis tip guide and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box.

From the manufacturer:

Uncover the mystery of your feline friend with this question and answer game created and written by experts for enthusiasts. Comes with 100 questions and answers, a 12 pages tip guide and scorepad in a sturdy flip top box.

Welcome to Monkey Business, the game of product development! You are the CEO of a small, but growing corporation. The goal of the game is to reach a certain number of bananas (money in the game) and a certain number of completed products in play, both based on the number of players in the game.

You have three types of workers (cogs in the great corporate wheel) to help you reach your goal: Engineering Monkeys, Marketing Monkeys, and Operations Monkeys. Each product in play requires an Operations Monkey to manage it. Engineers are allocated to products each turn to advance development. You collect bananas every turn for finished products in play. On a product's initial release, it gets a push from the marketing department, increasing sales on that turn, so the more Marketing Monkeys you have, the higher your initial sales. Meanwhile, your opponents are hiring their own monkeys and developing products at break-neck speeds. Using action cards and VPs will help you gain an advantage over them (allowing you to crush them mercilessly, in fact). Good luck creating your corporate empire!
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A game in the tradition of Happy Families but this has sets in varying numbers of cards. I expect parents saw an educational value in the game and they were right to do so.

There are 9 sets in all, each giving examples of a type of measure. The sets range from 3 cards to 7 cards. In your turn, you may ask any other player for a card in one set but you must have at least one card from that set in your hand. If you complete a set, lay it in front of you. When all the sets have been collected, the player who has most in front of him, wins.

The nine sets are:
Cubic Measure

The game can be played with questioning and answering by points. 10 points are the final goal, or it can also be played by time. Who has the most points after a certain time wins.

71 entertaining and educational riddles about movies. Three answers to each question. Answer correctly and get the card as a point. Player with the most points at the end of the game is the winner.

A novel target game. The target ,so-to-speak,is a clown holding up 5 hoops. Each hoop is worth points, the amount of points depends on the size of the hoops. The two smallest are worth 50 points each, the middle-sized hoop is worth 25 points and the two largest hoops are worth 10 points each.

To score these points you must launch the paper hat pieces one at a time. You place a hat on one end of a springboard in the shape of a clown. Hold down the end where the hat is and let go. The hat will be sprung forward, hopefully at the target. If it goes through a hoop you score the appropriate points.

The player with the most points when the game has to be finished, wins.

Klee made this game for the export market because they used their Clover logo on the rules (but the traditional Klee logo on the box. Klee is the German word for Clover).

description: Gladiatori: The Girl with the Long Sword is a miniature available with preorders of Gladiatori or as a promotional item at Spiel 2012 or the Lucca Games 2012 Fair. A fairy-tale journey for up to six Princes. It is a familiar Game of Goose type game, where you make your way along the track, obeying special square instructions should you land on one. It does have some innovations. Princes all start on different tracks at first for instance. And it has special rules for negotiating certain paths and, unusually, a river.

The large lithographed board is very nicely illustrated. Sadly, no publisher wanted to identify himself with the game, nor is an artist credited.

The actual rules:

THE NEW GAME
PRINCE’S QUEST
(PROTECTED).
This Game depicts 6 Princes in search of a Princess.
The various experiences of the Princes on their journeys embody the chief events culled from all the best known Fairy Tales.
INSTRUCTIONS.
1.—Any number up to the number of Princes can play. The choice of Roads to be determined by lots.
2.—Players start at the Milestone and proceed, in turn, along their respective roads, according to the throw of the dice.
3.— When a player falls on a division coloured a little heavier than the road, this is either an advantage or penalty, and directions will be found near the illustration bearing the number of the division.
4.— When the end of a Road is reached, the player goes on to the next as indicated on the board.
5.— The Road of Fortune must only be taken when the Fairy Godmother is met with, and must be again left if the division a piece falls on indicates it. The River of Darkness must be left as soon as a Landing Place is touched.
6— If a piece falls on a division already occupied, the pieces change places.
7.— The player who reaches No. 200 first is the winner of the game.

British Manufacture.

Players take the role of a beaver family trying to dam the river before any of their neighbors can do so.  The first to dam the river wins!

In this fast paced, family friendly card game, players build their dam by using big log cards to build the base layer of their dam.  Then they can add in some twig filler by playing twig filler cards.  Finally it can be topped off with mud by playing the appropriate mud cards.  When you use these building material cards, you then add cubes to you player mat, which represents your dam.

But watch out!  Other players can attack your dam and damage it by playing stone, weeds, or felled tree cards!  These attack cards cause you to lose cubes from your dam.  But if you're lucky, you'll have some helpful beaver cards in your hand that can save the day.  Using a Busy Beaver card you can add a few cubes to your dam, which gets you that much closer to winning!

This game is full of interaction and confrontation.  Build your dam, damage your opponents.  The first player to build their dam up and put all the necessary cubes on their player mat is the winner!

User Summary

Fantasy Armies is set of Generic Table Top Fantasy Wargames rules for 25/28mm fantasy miniatures.

The rules are to enable players to fight large battles or skirmish engagments with thier collections of fantasy miniatures.  They was a set of core rules (game engine) which every set of miniatures rules needs which deals with elements like the characteristics of your troops, their weapons, their morale and how these affect their performance on the tabletop like how to resolve combat or will they run away.

Fantasy Armies was written by Nick Eyre for Harlequin/Icon Miniatures for their range of metal miniatures; "Fantasy Battles" and Lord of the Rings Miniatures ranges (probably written to replace thier earlier rules; Raven).

The rules was to be published in a series of articles in Harlequin/Icon Miniatures own house magazine; The One Ring.  The core rules was to be published in three parts and the Army Lists would follow. The magazine only lasted 6 issues before the company change hands and the magazine was cancelled, so only the first two parts of the rules was published in issues 5 & 6.

In each round, one player is the ghost who wants to leave valuable items to the new dwellers in the castle. But where? Cards give clues, then the ghost hides the key somewhere in the room, in any place matching the clues. The player who finds it gets a key card. If the ghost makes a mistake, letters are handed out to the "victims" of the round. After 5-8 rounds, the player with most keys and letters is the winner.

IntroductionBrique is a drawless connection game for two players: Black and White. It's played on the squares of a checkered board, which is initially empty. The top and bottom edges of the board are colored black; the left and right edges are colored white.

				
				
					DefinitionsThe escorts of a light square are the square immediately in front of it and the square immediately to the left of it.

The escorts of a dark square are the square immediately behind it and the square immediately to the right of it.

				
				
					PlayBlack plays first, then turns alternate. On his turn, a player must place one stone of his color on an empty point.

After a placement, if the two escorts of one or more squares are occupied by friendly stones and said squares aren't occupied by friendly stones yet, the player must place a stone of his color on each one of them, removing, where appropriate, any enemy stones already placed there. Note that some squares on the edge of the board have only one escort or no escorts at all. These squares are not affected by this rule.

The game is won by the player who completes a chain of orthogonally adjacent stones of his color touching the two opposite board edges of his color. Draws are not possible.

				
				
					Pie ruleThe pie rule is used in order to make the game fair. This means that White will have the option, on his first turn only, to change sides instead of making a regular move.

				
				
					NotesTwo like-colored, diagonally adjacent stones parallel to the NE-SW diagonal are always connected through a common neighbor in Brique, so, as in Hex, each square has actually six neighbours, and the board behaves like a rhombus, with one diagonal twice as shorter as the other. Needless to say, however, Brique is not isomorphic to Hex.

boardgame: Sesame Street Mother Goose Audio Card Game
description:
This is a simple card game for preschool children with a cute gimmick... the package includes a cassette tape of the Sesame Street characters reading classic nursery rhymes.

Cards representing these nursery rhymes are distributed among the players. When the matching fairy tale (Humpty Dumpty, Jack Sprat, Old Mother Hubbard, etc.) is read by a character the kid holding that card tosses it away.

The children gain and lose cards during a session because the spinner is twirled when the stories appear on the tape. The player holding the Mother Goose card at the end of the game is declared the winner.

There are no decisions to be made and there is no strategy. This is a basic introduction to card games for three year old kids.

Includes 12 cards, spinner, cassette tape, plastic card holder, 
and the rules.

Designed for 2-4 players.

boardgame: Vermont Horror Expansion (fan expansion for Arkham Horror)
description:
I think I am going crazy. It may be that all I have ever written you is a dream or madness. It was bad enough before, but this time it is too much. They talked to me last night - talked in that cursed buzzing voice and told me things that I dare not repeat to you...

Vermont Horror is an unofficial fan-created expansion to Arkham Horror, based on the H.P. Lovecraft story 'The Whisperer in Darkness'. It introduces several new game concepts to the Arkham Horror Series, including a Prequel stage of the game.

Vermont is home to the Mi-go, a race of aliens from Yuggoth intent on plundering the earth of its natural resources, extracting valuable minerals from parallel planes underneath the earth. Whilst digging down to these inter-dimensional portals the Mi-go are inadvertently disturbing ancient seals buried deep in the ground. These eons old seals are holding the Ancient Ones at bay and as they become disturbed it increases the chances of the Old One stirring from its slumber.

Vermont Horror also adds one new Herald, a unique deck of Prequel events, over 30 new monster markers, plenty of new encounters from Townshend to the Dark Mountains, a deck of unique Mi-go technology to be harnessed and an expansion board featuring the locality of Vermont.

description:

There are 2 questions in each card with 3 answers for each questions. The winner is whoever can answer most questions correctly and thus can collect the most cards.
If the current player answers incorrectly the next player may risk with one of his collected card to answer it.

Very often spectacular scientific inventions are based upon nature - the aeroplane, roof tiles, or the newest thing, the "lotus effect" - a dirt-rejecting ability that now is used for sinks. With the simple means of a dice/movement game those connection between nature and science are explored and explained. Players try to find as many logical connections between nature and science as possible, the board parcours has 5 kinds of spaces, which require different forms of problem solving to find a solution. Players roll the die, advance their pawn and solve the problem of the space they land on. For a correct solution players receive a bionic-chip, who has collected five of them, wins the game.

Would you know what a fly and a bicycle have in common? The gear shift in a bicycle was devised from the fly's shoulder joint.

The title of this wargame for kids, published in Italy in 1940, means "Air-Naval War".

The game board has identical red and blue territories on each end.  The players follow a numbered line across the ocean to attack the other player based on a roll of the dice. Units can end up in the space which leads to combat. Each turn, each player must move one of their planes, submarines and ships (they have two of each in their starting armies), alternating until one of each unit type has moved. Players try to destroy each others units and bombard the enemy territory. This occurs when a unit ends its movement in an enemy occupied space. An airplane can bombard the wooden representations of the Fuel Dump, Ammo Dump, Rail Station, Bridge, Office, etc. and remove them as "booty".  The units must always move in one direction (toward the enemy) until they reach the end of the path, at that point each unit is treated differently (planes fly backward to the first square and ships are immediately returned to the 1st square and start moving toward the enemy again).

The Mas (torpedo boat) is an exception, it can move in both directions and sink any ship or plane that enters its space (it is only available to a player after that player has lost 3 units).
  
The island in the middle can be captured and fortified.  Victory occurs with one player destroys both enemy planes and half their ships.  There are no land based units except for an anti-air cannon which is placed in any empty, friendly land space at the beginning of the game.  It automatically destroys an airplane that ends it move in the same space.  Strategy comes from the choice of units to move each turn and becomes more involved with the activation of the Mas units.   It was released on June 10th, 1940, the day Italy enters World War 2.

Under Siege is an abstract game played on a circular board by 2 players. One player attempts to move his King piece from the center and completely off the board. The other player attempts to capture the King piece.

Turn based play where pieces move one space per move and capture opposing pieces by moving onto the space occupied by the piece being attacked.

Special moves apply to the larger pieces, namely being able to 'Vault' other pieces of the same team effectively allowing movement of two spaces under specific circumstances.

The King piece cannot capture opposing pieces.

The numbers on each side are uneven, the 'Peasants' have 16 pieces (8 smaller, 8 Larger) while the 'Royalty' have the King and 12 pieces (8 smaller, 4 Larger). This is to balance the fact that the 'Peasant' pieces start the game spread completely around the board, while the 'Royal' pieces are grouped together in the center.

Quest: Zeit der Helden - Wolfsjagd, the first thematic extension for Quest: Zeit der Helden - Angriff der Orks, offers an exciting new adventure and provides the Questmaster with many other figures of his own stories. This time, the heroes fall into the clutches of a terrible werewolf – and must keep themselves from succumbing to the werewolf's curse.

A new heroine supports the players in pursuit of the beast, as well as ten wolves and ten robbers, the most villains ever in a Quest game! Luckily, the heroes have access to new magic items specifically for hunting werewolves.

The strength of both the heroes and the Questmaster will continue to grow with each quest, the players continuing with their heroes from the original game. This personal development across multiple games will continue in future expansions.

A first introduction to calculations for very young children. The centerpiece of the game is a plastic octopus with two arms stretched out. Around it, fishes of various values (and weights) are placed. The active player picks a large bundle of fish and hangs it on one of the arms, then tries to find several individual fishes for the other arm. If successful, octopus Mr. Tom looks straight and the arms are balanced, if not, it is losing focus and balance. 
In the two player version, one child can pick one side and let the other player make the calculations for getting the octopus back into its balance.

Pinch It! is a simple dice game based primarily on luck. The game was intriguing since it was played using a 4x4 grid of pegs. The board is divided into four quadrants each having a different color. Within each quadrant is a 2x2 grid of pegs.

There is no significance to the grids. The gist is that every player has four pegs. You could just as easily play the game with each player having four rocks.

The die used is a six sided die with special markings which control the action of the player.


     Either of the 2 "Blank" faces – The player’s turn is over and the die is passed to the next player.



     "Hollow Square" face – The player discards one of his own pegs.



     "Hollow Triangle" face –  The player may take a peg from any player of his choice and discard it.



     "Solid Square" face – The player may replace one of his empty peg holes with a discarded peg. If he doesn’t have any empty hole, then his turn is finished.



      "Solid Square  and Hollow Triangle" face - The player may replace one of his empty peg holes with a  peg from any player of his choice. If he doesn’t have any empty hole, then his turn is finished.


The objective is to have the last peg on the board.

Bluffjack is a hybrid of poker and blackjack.  It borrows the bluffing and betting against other players from poker, and the trying to get close to 21 without going over from Blackjack (21).

In the game, players are dealt one card face down, and one card face up, and players wager.  Then players get cards, and try to get close to 21.  After this players will engage in another round of betting.

Bluffjack offers the chance for players to win if a player went over 21 and everyone else folded.  However, if all players remaining players went over 21, everyone loses, and their best are set aside.

You are a boxer.  You don't punch anybody; you work in a box making boxes for Bach's Boxes. The Bach family has made big bucks in the box business. They live in luxury based on the hard work of all their employees.  It's a dreary job with no advancement, but the job has one perk: The Bach's love entertainment. They buy the best tickets for every ball game, every play, every movie, every concert, every opera, and absolutely every show in town. They buy so many tickets that they always have tickets they can't use, which they give to the workers. Your objective is to schmooze the other employees to score tickets so you can party with your friends. The bigger the party, the more your friends will think of you. That's important, because right now your friends think of you as someone who makes minimum wage sitting in a box making boxes.

Players have employee cards and office cards.  First everyone simultaneously chooses and reveals an employee, then everyone simultaneously selects and reveals an office.  The employees go to the office where they were assigned.  Employees beat employees via a rock-paper-scissor mechanism.  The winning employee scores tickets for the player.

In Yggdrasil: Asgard, an expansion for Yggdrasil, each God can go into a Trance for one action. Once in a Trance, a God can neither fight in Asgard nor use his usual power – but all the Gods, including the one in Trance, receive a Trance power. Two examples of Trance powers: the Gods can discard the vikings' souls after rolling the die in combat, or they automatically remove five Fire giants from the bag of their choice while doing an action in the Kingdom of Fire. For one action, the entranced God can leave the Trance, at which point he recovers his usual power and can fight again.

Yggdrasil: Asgard contains twelve double-sided God tiles, with a God's usual power on one side and the Trance power on the other. To change a God's state, the player simply flips the tile. The Gods included in this expansion are:


    Six from the base game: Odin, Tyr, Thor, Frey, Freyja, and Heimdall
    Two from the promotional cards: Frigg and Vidar
    Four new Gods: Magni, Sif, Njord and Idunn

A pair of abstract games, made cheaply and aimed at children. Cross Over is a proper game, even adults will like it, but the rules for the Bridge Building game are completely indecipherable. So, I will only describe the Cross Over game.

Each player starts at opposite corners on the board. The object is to get one piece on to the opponents back corner (marked with an X on the board).

A die dictates distance of movement and you can only move a piece either horizontally or vertically. It would appear that you cannot change direction within the move. If you land on an opponent's piece, that piece is removed from the board. If a person has lost pieces and rolls a 6, he can bring on one of the lost pieces but only within his start field.

The game is magnetic with the two boards forming the top and bottom of a case and the pieces can be stored within that case.

In the game CO₂, each player is the CEO of an energy company responding to government requests for new, green power plants. The goal is to stop the increase of pollution, while meeting the rising demand for sustainable energy — and of course profiting from doing so. You will need enough expertise, money, and resources to build these clean power plants. Energy summits will promote global awareness, and allow companies to share a little of their expertise, while learning still more from others.

The Arctic is especially vulnerable to the effects of global warming, as has become apparent in the melting sea ice in recent years. Climate models predict much greater warming in the Arctic than the global average.

"CO₂: Promo Expansion Arctic" adds the arctic's area in game, a global Event - The Arctic Melts, and The Arctic Company Goal card.

By the way, if the pollution isn't stopped, it's game over for all of us.

In this game, players get to stack plastic barrels and planks on a slowly rotating base against the clock.

The pile will tumble when the base stops rotating and you score for how much you had constructed before the collapse.

The one player game is basically "See if you can beat your best score" type of thing. Much better as a competitive game.

Battery operated.

From the rules booklet: "A minature game (like) snooker with a difference.  A game designed for 2-4 players from 8yrs to adult.  A game as thoughtful as chess played with the tenacity of skillful snooker players."

Additional sets of player tiles in blue and green to expand the base "For The Win" game (2 players) to a maximum of 4 players.

This was a special item for Kickstarter project backers.  However, TMG reserved the right to distribute some additional copies of this expansion as promos.

This is the 4x4 version of the game Connex'Oh which uses a grid of 6x6 tiles and was also released by EdiGames.

2 players face each other across a large plastic board that contains a grid of 4x4 square plastic tiles. All tiles are divided into a black and white triangular half, with a landing space on each side for the playing piece of that color. Tiles have a handle stuck in the middle that enables them to be lifted up, twisted round and replaced without the risk of having pieces slip off. Both the black and white player have 4 stones of their respective colors that start on opposite sides of the board. All tiles initially face the same direction, so that no two touching sides have the same color and hence no "connections" exist on the board at the start of play.

By twisting tiles, players can create "connections" of identically colored halves, creating a corridor along which pieces of that color can progress. Each turn players either twist one tile in the aim to improve the available route, or move a stone along one of those routes. Players try to remove all stones in their color from the other side of the board. The first to do so is the winner. A tile that has just been lifted gets a red marker on it. It prevents that tile from being turned by the player who is next. Additionally, it also prevents 1 or 2 neighboring tiles from being turned, as long as a connection in that player's color was created to the neighboring tile(s) by the newly placed one. An elegant rule that prevents stalemates and adds another tactic to employ.

This comes in a book called "If You Were There: Viking Times."  It is for 2 or 3 players.  The map extrends from about the Ural Mountains to Vinland (Canada).  The object of the game is to collect trade tokens which are acquired by moving from space to space along a track on the map as directed by the roll of a die.  Some of the trail squares have further instructions like "miss a turn" or "lose a token."  Each player also begins with "life" tokens which can be lost along the way.  Lose all 3 and you are out of the game.

The unique feature of the game is that it lasts for exactly 23 minutes so a timer must be provided.  The amount of time represents 5 seconds for each year from A.D. 793 to 1066.  This obviously makes for a very fast game.

The drawback of the game is that the movers and tokens and die as well as the timer must be provided by the players.  They can use buttons or coins or parts from other board games.

Koschupa is a hand-crafted three-player-game by Dieter Zander from Historische Spiele Zander.

Koschupa is short for "KOsakenSCHUbs-PArty" (jostling-cossacks-knees-up).

It's an Finger Flicking Game. The board is a circle whose outer parts are subdivided into the three player-starting-positons. Each player starts with nine pieces in his colour at the outermost of the board.

You flick your pieces to kick the opponents' pieces off board. If you manege to kick at least one opponent's piece off board while your own piece stays on the board, you can flick again. After the muximum of three consecutive flicks, your turn is over. The next one's turn starts.
If you're "Last-Man-Standing" you start the next round with eight instead of nine pieces. Your starting position will be one circlet further to the centre of the board. If you've reached the fourth circlet and you win the next round, you've won the game.

Scrum is a pure abstract board game that roughly remind Rugby.

Each player has a number of pieces that remain for the whole game (there is no capture). In addition, a special piece, the ball, is present on the board.

The goal is to bring the ball to the last row. Typically each player move consists of 2 player pieces movement. However, in special occasions, when the ball is surrounded by player pieces so that there only one exit cell, the player is only allowed one movement which consists of pushing the ball to this exit.

Even if the rules are very simple (one minute is generally enough to understand how to play), it is tactically and strategically deep.

Online Play


     Jocly (turn-based, live play or play against the computer)

This is the google Translation of the german game description:

In several rounds of play, the Knights try individually or jointly to the respective places on the board to achieve victory. Failing that, they get handkerchiefs as favors and can move onto the barrel fields of the board towards the castle. Whoever reaches the castle, and has the most favorable evidence, winner of the rivalry.

In the middleage the players fights to get points to get to the castle. There are some fights I have to solve alone, other fights must be solved by all the players together. The player with the most points win the game.

Market Meltdown is set in a time of financial chaos, when the general public have lost faith in the Bank's ability to safely house their deposits and are withdrawing their money and transferring it to their mattresses. The players represent the traders investing the money on behalf of the Bank. As the game progresses players are faced with ever increasing pressure to generate bigger returns to pay back deposits and stay afloat.

The winner is the player who remains afloat when his opponents have gone bust. To begin, each player takes 900 million from the Bank and places their private jet on the Market Opens square. The Bank’s depositors are represented by a red sterling currency piece that also starts on the board, one square in front of the jets.

Players pay back their depositors at the start of every trading day. 25 million is due from each player on the first trading day, and the amount due doubles every day. Each player rolls the dice on his turn, and then moves both his private jet and the Depositors piece in a clockwise direction around the board for the number of squares indicated by the dice. A new trading day starts when the Depositors piece lands on or passes the Market Opens square.

The stock market is represented by a roulette wheel, and the roulette board is printed on the middle of the game board. Players go to market by playing roulette, with the returns on their bets being the same as those in traditional roulette. When the players land on a Data square, they collect market data, represented by stoppers. When going to market, players can place the stoppers in the roulette wheel, reducing the amount of open numbers and thus improving the odds of winning. Players still get the same return as in traditional roulette if they win. Players must contend with a number of other squares in the course of a trading day, including Data Wipe, Go Rogue, Arbitrage, Financial Regulator, Luck, and Banker’s Bonus squares.

The Drum Roll: Mini Expansion consists of five new performer cards (two copies of each) and seven new personnel cards. The performers are:


    The Acrobat troupe gains a Balancing Man, the first Acrobat to retire for 3 points.
    The Tamers troupe gains a Bear Tamer, who retires for 4 points and lets you get cubes by paying money.
    The Jugglers troupe gains a Ball Juggler.
    Magicians gain a Cabinet Magician who has flexible performance colors, but costs 5 for hire and upkeep.
    The new Bizarre act is a Sword Eater who is the first 5-cost-for-hire Bizarre act, but still retires for 1 x the number of Bizarre acts.
Drum Roll: Dragon Dancers is a promotional card available at Spiel 2012. The Dragon Dancers belong to a new performer type called "Oriental"; the card is unique and has a new ability.

boardgame: Masters' Series: Adventure 1 – The Deadly Hand of Zargon (fan expansion for HeroQuest)
description:
The biggest challange to making these games has always been, How to keep it fresh and new. After making the 8th game in the adventure series, My Heroes wanted very much to keep the mercanaries. The quests got to big for the original board. So we created a bigger board. My first Son wanted more fighting, my second Son wanted more treasure, and I wanted more furniture. Add to this all the monsters, cards, tiles and rules from all the expansions and we created the Masters' Series. This is a very exciting adventure that we're sure your going to love. It will require a bit of work on your part to create the board and tiles. You will need to buy quite a few extra monsters and furniture for the game. But all of this is provided for you in the making a Masters' Series game booklet. 
The pages for this adventure are available to download at "Dragonkingdoms.co.uk" and yahoo's group site "HeroQuest- hero quest" for free. You do need to join the group to access the file's section where they are stored. The photo section of this site will allow you to read the story and look at the maps to see if this game is something you would like to play. Your adventure awaits!

Third Person Shooter (or TPS for the acronym-hungry) is basically a tactical First Person Shooter transmogrified into a tabletop game. The intent was to have as few rules as possible, to avoid them getting in the way of the fragfest. TPS can be taught in under 5 minutes, and a game round usually takes less than 25 minutes. TPS scales well, and runs with little or no lag with 8 to 10 players!

TPS uses simultaneous movement and combat with a minimum of fuss and constraints, such as hexes or movement allowances. As such TPS has been accused of thinking far outside the conventional box for tabletop games.

TPS pits the Terrorists against the counterterrorists in the classic struggle for most frags and points to buy cool upgrades and fancy equipment. Supported game modes in v.1.0 are Hostage-rescue and Bomb placement/disarmament.

More features will come with future patches and major versions.

The future of TPS is currently uncertain - But in a good way - We are looking into doing a redesign and getting it published :)

This expansion for Fantastiqa lets you become an Adventurer for Hire! Fantastiqa is required for you to play.

Components:


    18 Special Delivery Cards (3 per terrain type; all 18 cards have unique flavor text)
    8 +1 Point Bonus Tokens


Special Delivery cards reward your Adventurers for performing escort and delivery missions to Regions all over Fantastiqa. Special Deliveries may not always be glamorous, but the pay is good!

Examples include:


    Return Five Stolen Chin Hairs to the Brooding Bridge Ogre Brenda!



    Deliver a Supply of Decaffeinated Brew to the Nervous Necromancers of Groo!



    Return the Forbidden Tome of Terrors -- Unopened! -- to the Lonely Librarian Lyria. (This book is overdue.)


Summary:
Each player begins the game with one Special Delivery card. Each card shows one of the six Regions in Fantastiqa (Fields, Forests, Frozen Wastelands, Hills, Highlands, or Wetlands). If you end your turn in a Region marked on your Special Delivery card, you complete the Special Delivery and add one Gem to your supply. You also keep the Special Delivery card (turn it face-down on your Adventurer Placard). Then redraw a new Special Delivery (you will always have one active Special Delivery card available.) Trade in three completed Special Delivery cards for one +1 Point Bonus Token.

In the Treasure Hunt Expansion for Fantastiqa, you stumble upon a curious collection of Treasure Maps. Use these maps—-and your dog-—to dig up mysterious treasures all over Fantastiqa! A new Victory Cup Quest is also added to the game (more on this exciting Quest below...)

Fantastiqa is required for you to play.

Components:

    12 Treasure Map cards (2 for each Region)
    14 Mystery Tokens (see details below)
    12 +1 Point Bonus Tokens


Summary:
At the beginning of the game, one Treasure Map card is hidden face-down underneath the Quest Goal card. This Treasure Map indicates the Region where the Victory Cup is located--a final Quest required for victory!

During the game, one Treasure Map card is always visible as an Open Quest for any player to fulfill. To fulfill a Treasure Map Quest, a player must travel to the Region showing on the Treasure Map and dig there (using his "Dig Dog"). On doing this, the player claims one +1 Point Bonus Token and one Mystery Token (see below). The Treasure Map card is discarded face-up at the bottom of the Treasure Map deck and a new Treasure Map card is displayed on top (reshuffle as needed).

When one player reaches the Quest Goal, the Victory Cup Quest tucked beneath the Quest Goal is revealed, face-up, for all to see. The first player who fulfills both of the following conditions wins the game: a) the player’s score meets or exceeds the Quest Goal, and b) the player uses a “Dig” symbol (on his Dog card) to unearth the Victory Cup from the Region showing on the card.

List of Mystery Tokens:


    Artifact: Draw one Artifact from the top of the Artifact deck and claim it for free. Immediately add it to your hand.
    Beast: Draw one Beast from the Beast deck and claim it for free. Immediately add it to your hand.
    Flying Carpet: Fly to any adjacent Region over a marked road. (Just like a regular Magic Carpet token.)
    Magic Compass: Travel to any Region diagonally adjacent to your own (i.e., across the terrain occupied by an Open Quest card).
    Pocket Portal: Teleport to any Region.
    Potion of Persuasion: Use the potion to subdue any adjacent Creature while Adventuring (move your Adventurer as usual). Immediately add this Creature to your hand.
    Secret Stash: The token is worth 4 Gems. These Gems do not count against you when the Mischievous Raven appears or when other players use Artifacts like the Rogue’s Purse. They cannot be stolen by the Mischievous Raven or by other players. Use these Gems just like regular Gems when purchasing items. No change is given.

This game is based on the comic strip Blondie.

Contents:

     Spinner with positions for Blondie, Dagwood, Alvin, Baby Dumpling, Mr. Dithers and Daisy.
     Playing board for each of Blondie, Dagwood, Alvin, Baby Dumpling, Mr. Dithers and Daisy.
     Paper chips used to mark positions on player's boards


The obect of the game is to score points based on play.

Each player's playing board has 12 positions on which he can mark a point. Imagine 4 corners of a square. Now on each side of the square there are two positions between the corners.

You can mark one of the positions on the card whose picture you spin. If you get 4 positions along a side you must say "Oh Blondie" to score points.

There are there are other ways to score points.

This game attempts to simulate a boxing match. Like a real boxing match the winner of each round is based on the number of points scored in that round. The winner of the game is determined by who wins the most rounds.

There are three spinners. The first spinner is used as a timer keeper to break each round into 6 parts (minutes:seconds) at 3:00, 2:30, 2, 1:30, 1 and 0:30.

The second spinner is used as such and indicates what happens with the punch. The third spinner is used as such too, and indicates the referee's decision when one is needed.

Fairland is sort of a "Chutes and Ladders" type game played on a board of 100 spaces. However there is a significant twist. If you land on the bottom of a chute then you get some token to play to get by the top of the chute. For example:


     At 9, you get meat to feed the lion
     At 19 you get blinding powder to throw at the tiger
     At 29 you must feed the pig a cabbage, but if you don't have a cabbage then you are sent back to space 4 to get one. 


The requirement to have the token on some of the spaces generates a lot more "churn" than in a regular "Chutes and Ladders" game.

This is a similar game to the well known Scrabble in that letters tiles are blind drawn and used to form words, and that the objective is to score points. However there are significant differences.

(1) The words are placed on table in a pool area rather than being in a crossword grid. So word two does not need to have a letter in common with word one.

(2) There are not points on the letters, but points are scored primarily based on the number of letters in the word.

(3) 350 letter tiles

(4) After each turn unused letters are put in a discard pool which subsequent players can used to make words.

Salamaua '42 is a solitaire simulation of the Australian commando raid of 29 June 1942 on the north coast of New Guinea.

The Australians must cause as much damage to the Japanese defenders while minimising their own casualties.

The action is regulated by a series of tables and the level of success is determined by the amount of risk the commandos are willing to take. The roll of the dice also makes the outcome unpredictable.

CONTENTS:
1 x colour map
180 x counters, mounted and ready-to-use 
2 x double-sided player aid cards
1 x set of rules
