description:
Victorious advance!

A new game from our Air Force by H. Kallenberger from the collection "Scholz's artistic games".

This game is adapted from real live and provides a fairly accurate picture of the use of air power. In it the combat units (bombers) are used to advance far into the enemy rear. These are heavy machines, suitable for carry large bomb loads over long distances.

The goal is to destroy the HQ of the opponent by hitting it with a bomber(only a bomber). Play also ends if the one of the player's HQ's has no place to move or if the player has lost all his bombers.

Sea Chess is a board game, like Chess, or Checkers.  Chess, however, is a game that interprets Medieval land warfare while Sea Chess interprets a modern Naval Battle.  Every Sea Chess game piece represents a warship.  Both players get 24 game pieces representing 3 battleships, 4 cruisers, 8 destroyers and 9 escorts.  Each type of "ship" moves freely about the board with its own characteristic motion, speed and direction.  The "ships" are engaged in dynamic moving action until sunk and you can watch all of the action.

In Sea Chess there is a gradual "sinking" instead of "jumping" or "taking" pieces.  A game piece moves into range and "fires".  A roll of the dice determines a successful hit.  Each type of "ship" has its own characteristic striking force and range.  Pegs are placed in the pieces to record successful hits.  When the holes are full, the piece has been "sunk".  Since a peg marks each successful hit, you can keep track of how much damage you are doing to your opponent's ships.

READY REFERENCE CARDS included with the game give you a neat summary to keep by your side as you play so you do not have to keep looking into the rule book for speed, direction, striking force, etc.

RANGE GAUGES are provided to help you determine what ships are in range and which are not.  Soon you will be able to tell just by looking which ships are in range.

You develop the strategy!  You make the plans!  You decide whether to make the first attack or prepare a devastating counter-attack!

From the game page on The Game Crafter

For too long the Meeples have lived under the boot of the mighty Regime. You have been pushed around and mistreated while the Regime grows rich off your toil. No longer! The day has come to rise up and take control of your fate. The day has come for Uprising!

Uprising is a semi-cooperative game for 2-4 players who take the role of rebel groups rising up against the vicious Regime. Will you be able to work together to overthrow your oppressors, or will you turn on the others to try to become number one?

Summary of Game Play

The board is a grid, where each cell ("sector") can be controlled by a player, by the regime, or by nobody. Each turn, players draw tiles indicating where they can take actions, such as attacking or reinforcing. After all the players have taken their turns, the regime takes 3 automated actions in random sectors. Each battle can be fought with military power, or with popularity.

The game ends when any player (or the regime) reaches a target score, based on popularity and sectors controlled. At that point, the force with the highest score wins, and if the regime is highest, all the players lose. In the standard rules, players can directly attack each other or temporarily align with the regime. There is a fully-cooperative variant, where players face a stronger regime.

Immortal is a strategy game of warring mythologies, combining area control and card battle play. The war of the gods has begun... Born from the dreams and nightmares of mankind, mythic beings are gathering forces to crush their enemies. They have put their fate in the hands of mere mortals like yourself, granting you the power to lead them to victory. Capture your opponents’ forces, turn their powers against them, and forge your own legend!

In Immortal, ancient gods, heroes, and monsters battle for control over mythic realms. 2 to 4 players assume command of powerful pantheons summoned from world mythologies - Master Set One features the Norse, Greek, Japanese, Celtic, Egyptian, and Native American pantheons. Each pantheon of character cards offers unique abilities and strategies to be mastered. Battles take place on a battlefield created from modular board tiles, representing the nexus of mythical realms. The goal of the game is to control the most cards on the battlefield by capturing your opponents’ cards and defending your own.

During the game, players take turns placing their character cards on the spaces of the board. Each card has a distinct combination of battle strengths, represented by numbers on its four edges. When a card is placed on a space adjacent to the north, south, east, or west edge of an opponent’s card, an attack begins and the strengths on the cards’ touching edges are compared. If the attacker’s strength is greater, then the defending card is successfully captured, giving the attacking player more control over the battlefield. Special abilities can influence the results of an attack, and set the board up for future combos by strengthening allies, weakening enemies, and bending the normal rules of the game.

After the first battle, players upgrade their pantheon decks with more powerful cards, and expand the battlefield with additional modular boards. Victory goes to the player who controls the most cards at the end of two battles. Game variants allow players to form teams, change the rules of engagement, or play faster matches. Once players have mastered the basic strategies of the game, they can mix and match characters from different pantheons to build their own custom decks. As Immortal evolves, more pantheons will be added from the myths and legends of distinct cultures. These expansions will introduce new boards and cards, and new abilities and playing styles to the game.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
A building game where players are acquiring all of the elements the need to build a three-story tower. Players use cards to buy equipment from the stock -- H-beams and joints create substructures, and V-beams are used to build to the next story.  The goal of the game is to be the first player to build a three-story building that consists of three substructures separated by V-beams, and a roof.

Game play consists of turns where players either buy materials, or use purchased materials to build building elements.  Unbuilt raw materials are vulnerable to card-play from other players that may require them to give over unused materials, or may cause a player to return all unused materials to the supply.  Additionally, some special action cards prevent a player from building until the player resolves a strike, either through the payment of money, or the play of a contract card.

From Spielbox Magazine:

Louis Armstrong is an additional leader for 7 Wonders: Leaders. It is only playable with 7 Wonders: Leaders, but may also be integrated along with the leaders of 7 Wonders: Cities.

At the end of the game, Louis gives 7 victory points minus the number of military victory tokens you have won.

boardgame: The People vs. O.J. Simpson Trivia Game
description:
Object of the Game: As an investigator, your job is leave your color start position and make your way, by answering questions, to the six locations on the map, and return to your position before your opponents do.

Possibly a prototype of a game that was to be sold and money raised was to be used for OJ Simpson's defense. The game may have been banned and/or rejected and never mass produced.

boardgame: Pointless Wacky Untested Game I Made Up One Afternoon
description:
INTRODUCTION:
Want to play a game that is literally untested, unproven, and that was made up one afternoon for a game contest?  You've come to the right place then!

This is a game that makes absolutely no sense, but you may end up having fun anyway, just for spite.

So, roll up your sleeves and let's get started!

DESCRIPTION:
This game is primarily a party or icebreaker game where people take turns to try to complete challenges.

GAME OBJECTIVE:
Be the player with the lowest score at the end of the game

GAMEPLAY:
Players each receive tokens (of 4 varying colors), and then each player takes turns completing challenge cards.  If a player is successful, they will generally reduce the number of tokens that they have (which are worth points).  If a player fails a challenge, they will gain tokens.  Each player is trying to gain the lowest score possible.  Challenges are each very unique, and can be physical, logical, luck, or knowledge-based.

GAME ELEMENTS:

Light-hearted gameplay
    Players can learn more about fellow players in a fun way
    Token point system keeps everyone involved

Similar electroni game than Electro but instead of questions-answers there are pictures. The two plug are connected to each end of the battery and when the plugs are placed on the two points that are connected (i.e. the pair of pictures belonging together) the lamp will shine to indicate this.

Electronic battery operated matching game has 10 different pages to play. When you match one picture on one side of the page to a picture on the other side of the page with the electric wands, a red light lights up at the bottom of the game to show that you are correct.

Player with the most correct answers is the winner.

Exercises Snake Game is a gambling game. Players would put up stakes (money) to play and then divide the stakes according to the outcome of the game.

The game notes that it is "to be played with a tee-totum, or with dice."

The game has a snakes with number 1 near head and number 54 near the tail. The snake is laid out in s curves along the vertical direction. he sections of the snake are in different colors to show Charles Magnus' printing ability. The page is also decorated with men exercising for the same purpose. They have no absolutely no function in the game.

The special positions on the snake are colored red and are have special instructions.

2  - Gives the player two throws more
   9  - 1 more throw
  20  - Go back to No. 4
  27  - Advance to 32.
  33  - 1 more throw
  40  - Gives the player one-half of all the stakes
  46  - Stop for the next turn
  52  - The player has lost and must cease to play
  52+ - Those who throw over 52 divide the balance
        of the stakes


More to "More"

This particular image found on the web was a sheet of paper which had the game on one side  and a handbill advertising Charles Magnus' wares on the other. The handbill side indicates that this is one of 12 such games printed on a paper sheet and available for purchase.

A children's game published in the French comic/magazine Francs Jeux.

The game has two boards. The first is a short Monopoly-style track. The aim is to land on squares featuring the six items that you need before you can try to fly to the moon. Movement is dictated by a die roll (you must supply your own die). The spaces you must land on to get these six things are:
T = Launching pad
F = Rocket
A = Apollo Capsule
L = Lunar landing module
J = Moon Buggy
E = Crew

When you have acquired these, you move to the second board. This is a traditional one track game (á la the Game of Geese). If you land on special spaces, you must obey the instructions. The first to get to the moon, wins.

Abstract two player game with a soccer theme. The player that scores more goals than the other after an agreed time wins.

Each player has a team consisting of 11 figures. The playing field consists of 83 spaces with connections between them. On each space there can only be one figure or the ball.

On each turn a player can kick the ball in a straight line if he has a player next to the ball and move one of his 11 figures one space. If a player cannot kick the ball he can move two figures one space each (or one figure twice).

There are some more rules like fouls, passing or kicking the ball over an opposing figure.

Objective: Be the first moving up to field 108
 - moving by turn -
throw the die and move according rolled number; ending on a marked space follow instructions glued on cover inside.

A dice and card game for two to four players. In your turn, you roll the dice and if you roll a total on one of your cards, you can discard it. If a player is dealt an "X-IT" card, it may be used as a wild card, i.e., 1 to 6. First to discard all their cards, wins. X-IT cards held by the losers, count as 6 points, other cards as at face value.

The aim of TOTEM is for your chief to build their totem pole before the other chiefs do, but it's not as easy as it sounds!

Players first decide how high totem poles need to be to win, between one and four totems, with higher poles meaning longer games.  Cards in TOTEM come in three colours, representing the spirit power associated with them - red = power of destruction, yellow = power of protection, green = power of creation.  Players are dealt at random a card representing their chief, and a spirit card (which is not looked at), which can be used as a risky last resort to destroy other player's totems, but can backfire and destroy yours (if the spirit card doesn't match the colour of the chief using it).

Players then take turns to turn over and collect totem part cards.  To build a totem, players need to collect three cards, a wood, tools and paint card, in the same colour as their chief.  Once they do this they discard those cards and choose a totem in their colour to add to their pole.  There are several totem designs for each colour, so players can customize the way their totem pole looks.

Some cards in the totem parts deck are Magic Items - if a player picks one of these up and it is in their chief's colour, they can use it to do various things, from destroying other players' hands of cards (the Tomahawk), to preventing other players using magic items (the Teepee), or allowing them to look at other player's cards and swap any they want to (Pipe of Peace).

Once a player has created the set number of totems, they need to finish off by creating a Totem Topper (collect a wood, tools and paint card in each of the three colours in any order), and then they win!  But play can continue to determine other placings.

Components:
Totem Parts (42 cards - wood, tools, paint, plus 6 Magic Items)
Totems (24 cards - 8 destruction, 8 protection, 8 creation, all double sided)
Chiefs (6 cards - 2 destruction, 2 protection, 2 creation)
Spirits (6 cards - 2 destruction, 2 protection, 2 creation)
Totem Toppers (3 cards, double sided)

Think Trunk is the perfect group and party game.  Its unique game play allows for everyone to be involved on every turn.

There are 5 different categories on each card: Think It, Number It, Act It, Prefer It, and Wing It.  All 5 categories encompass and expand on current games on the market but are adapted for multi-player input without teams.

The object of the game is to be the first player to get to the end and a single round takes 20-30 minutes.

Roll the Master Die to determine the "Point", then its off to the races as everybody rolls their 3 dice at once to score first!  Roll High, Low, Odds, Evens, 3 of a Kind, or a Straight to win.

Roll the Master Die to determine what everyone is rolling for: odds, evens, three low, three high, low straight, or high straight.  Players all roll at the same time and each player may "hold out" die as they roll the correct number. For example if you are going for "odds" and you roll a 1, you can place it aside and keep rolling to get the 3 and the 5.  First player to get the correct roll, moves his/her marker along the score track.  First to the finish, wins.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: Red Guards Anti-Tank Squad – "Red Thunder"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Anti-tank squads are an essential element of modern infantry formations, and the Dust Tactics: Red Guards Anti-Tank Squad are a core element of the famed Red Guards Infantry Regiments. These veteran soldiers have spent countless hours perfecting their use of heavy weapons and aren't afraid to get up close and personal with the world's biggest war machines. If they get into range, there’s nothing they can't destroy.

These anti-tank specialists come equipped with two PTRS-47 bazookas, each capable of punching a hole through even the most heavily armored walkers. Deploy these soldiers to add some spine and versatility to any Dust Tactics SSU army!

boardgame: Dust Tactics: Red Guards Command Squad – "Red Command"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Long behind the Axis and Allies in VK technology and combat walkers, the SSU army of Dust Tactics has relied on the strength of its infantry to endure the war, and Soviet field officers have been no strangers to their nation's suffering. Accordingly, though the Red Guards Command Squad offers SSU players all the standard Command Squad skills, allowing them to reactivate your other units, call in artillery strikes, and help coordinate your attacks, these stalwart veterans arrive at the battlefield better armed than their Axis and Allied counterparts. This squad means business!

These battle-tested soldiers have upgraded their weaponry to better combat any direct threat they might encounter. The Red Guards Command Squad carries UGLs and a PTRS-47 for the times when it's best to lead by example. After all, these soldiers are more than just a group of tough officers and specialists: They are First Red Guards.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: IS-5 Heavy Tank – "Mao Zedong / Vladimir Lenin"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

With less access to VK-driven technologies, SSU engineers have had to invent other means to combat the powerful walkers deployed by their Axis and Allied foes. Their response? The Dust Tactics: IS-5 Heavy Tank comes equipped with serious SSU firepower in both of two Damage Resilient configurations.

Built to deal with serious threat of enemy aircraft, the Mao Zedong controls a battlefield at range with its Quad 85 mm AA Gun. These powerful guns are deadly to enemy aircraft and can be used effectively against ground targets as well.

Meanwhile, the SSU has long considered artillery the most important weapon on the modern battlefield. Since the beginning of the war, fielding these huge guns has proven a successful tactic for the Soviets, one which they see no reason to stop now. The second configuration of the IS-5, the Vladimir Lenin, is a powerful artillery platform, capable of reducing enemy targets to rubble with its massive 252 mm field mortar.

Objective: Be the first moving up to field 80
- moving by turns -
throw the die and move according rolled number; ending on a red marked space follow instructions.

Objective: Be the first moving up to field 70
- moving by turn -
throw the die and move according rolled number; ending on a red marked space follow instructions.

Operational level wargame for the battle of Midway in World war 2 Pacific theater.
This game published with Japanese history and military magazine "Rekishi Gunzo" #114 the Twenty Anniversary Issue. And also published along with Tsushima.

Players control either Imperial Japanese fleet or United States fleet.
Each player choose and resolve one of seven actions alternately. The seven actions is search, deck work, air sortie, resolve of air raid, mission abort, repair and additional damage check.
Unlike many other games, the grid map and unit movement are not include in this game. The game is progress by the viewpoint of the fleet commander.

Winner is determined by the damage given to enemy fleet and airbase each other, but Japanese player must given at least 6 points of damage for Midway airbase.

Not confirmed on the box, but this must have been a promotional game for the National Trust, maybe to sell in their souvenir shops exclusively.

It is a simplicated table golf game, with marbles acting as golf balls and a cheap plastic golf hole, a flag (to add realism perhaps!) and a club for each player.

Set the hole somewhere on the table and whack a marble with your club. The fewest shots taken, wins.

I think you will need a spirit-level because slightly sloping tables and marbles don't go together very well.

Game is housed in a plastic pouch rather than a box.

boardgame: Kiyomori Gunki: Hogen and Heiji Rebellion
description:
Issue game of Wargame Japanese History #12.

Operational level wargame on Hogen and Heiji rebellions during Japanese Heian period.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hōgen_Rebellion
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heiji_Rebellion
This game include two scenarios for each rebellion and two type campaigns for combined rebellions.
A "gunki" is a war story (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gunki_monogatari).
Kiyomori is a famous Heian period person.

The game map covers city of Kyoto for movement and combat. It also includes main part of Japan for reinforcement march.
Each unit represents a lord (daimyo) or samurai warriors (retainers). All units has a battle rating and belong to one of the four clans. The clan is affect for unit activation and special ability. Certain lords (daimyo) have also special abilities.

Players control one side of each rebellion. Most side contains more than one clan.
Each turn players determine active clan by die roll. Activated unit are flipped down at end of their activation. So each unit can only activate once per turn, but Fujiwara clan's special ability allows them to reactivate flip down units.

Winner is determined by several victory condition per scenario. For example, elimination of highest lord, occupation of important areas, etc.
family: History: Conflicts of the Heian period – Japan

description:
This is a Germany-themed BrainBox game.

Description provided by the publisher:

Do you know which regions the river Rhine flows through Or where the Nürburgring is? BrainBox All Around Germany contains 69 beautifully-illustrated cards showing the very best Germany has to offer, and then some! The object of the game is to study a card for 10 seconds and then answer a question based on the roll of a die. If the question is answered correctly, the card is kept and the person with the most number of cards after 5 or 10 minutes is the winner. Made from a minimum of 70% recycled material, the BrainBox range is fabulous fun. Find out who the BrainBox is in your family – it may not be who you think it is!

This is a France-themed BrainBox game.

Description provided by the publisher: Do you know where Mont St Michel is? Or where Camembert comes from? BrainBox All Around France contains 53 beautifully-illustrated cards showing the very best France has to offer, and then some! The object of the game is to study a card for 10 seconds and then answer a question based on the roll of a die. If the question is answered correctly, the card is kept and the person with the most number of cards after 5 or 10 minutes is the winner. Made from a minimum of 70% recycled material, the BrainBox range is fabulous fun. Find out who the BrainBox is in your family – it may not be who you think it is!
Objective: Be the first moving up to field 100- moving by turn - throw the die and move according rolled number; ending on a marked space follow instructions.

London Brodge is a simple marble shooting game for points. "London Bridge" in this case is a pasteboard picture of the bridge with four arches. From left to right the arches score 1, 4, 3, and 2 points.

From the box top:

Directions
Place the Bridge on the floor with the supports behind.
The game is for each player to shoot the marbles in turn, those passing under the arches score the number thereon. 
The player making the highest score wins.

From the rule sheet

Wake up your environmental Protection consciousness

The Nature is easy to learn and quick to play.  It's best for four or more players, though you can play with three people or more than five.  The game aims to help players developing the consciousness of environment protection through fun and enjoyment.

Object to the game:
The object is to get living animal cards as many as you can in each round.  Points score on the cards in player's "Zoo" card pile but minus points in the player's "Tomb" card pile.

Game play:
Player started with 5 cards.  Each round he can 1) play a card; 2) draw a card and discard a card into his "Tomb" card pile.

When every player has play one card, the round is over.  The greatest card of the latest colour will be the winner of that round.  The winner can collect all the cards and place them into his "Zoo" card pile.

Game end when the last card is drawn from the deck.

The world's first 14 sided bowling ball.

The fascinating Tetrakaideccahedronnocube... Just call me "TET."

There are 10 frames to each game and the player who has the highest score at the end wins. Each player has to complete frame 1 before the play moves on to frame 2 and so on. Maximum score per two rolls is 10. A spare is 10 points. A white dot on the first roll counts as a strike and no second roll is taken. It is pretty straight forward.

Equipment sold as a unit for playing a game simulating bowling by TET Leisuretime Concepts, Inc. in Arcadia, CA, 91006.

Similar games:

    Bowling Dice
    Bowl and Score
    Bowl-A-Strike
    Spare Time Bowling

Terapia

It is composed of a quad-fold board, the rules are printed on the back side of the box, 6 pawns (black, orange, red, blue, green, yellow) and 600 question cards. Themed on medical science, all questions are about medical (but not psychological) affairs.

The game goal is to be the first to move your pawn along the complete circle on the board from your starting color rectangle to the same position. There is a single pile of cards in the center of the board. Rectangles and cards are identified with 3, 2 or 1 dots. The player at your right asks you the question on the upper card of the pile. You must give right answer to the card question to be able to advance a single rectangle or 3 or 2 if dots stated in your actual position match the current question card dots. That has a maximum of three consecutive advances. Then the turn goes to the player at your right. If you fail a question the pawn moved is the one belonging to the player who asked the question.

boardgame: Empires of the Void: Key to the Universe
description:
On every inhabited world in the known galaxy, the Tolmek appeared, a race of pan-dimensional beings of immense power. Everywhere their message was the same: "We are dying and must choose a successor." The Tolmek are the keepers of the Key to the Universe, a powerful artifact that holds the secrets of their power. They will give this Key to the race that proves themselves most capable and worthy. The Tolmek are watching. Will you inherit the Key to the Universe?

The small expansion Empires of the Void: Key to the Universe is designed to make the game more fun and balanced. Players now have a fourth action each round that can be used to perform new actions. One of the new actions is called Enslave, which increases the income of conquered planets. Also included are technology boards, which replace tech tokens and have a number of new technologies that open up new paths to victory. Four additional alien races provide more choices for players. Key to the Universe also has a new, revised rulebook for Empires of the Void that makes the game easier to learn and more clearly explains how to play.
  
Key to the Universe, currently in beta-testing, is available as a free downloadable, print-and-play expansion here:

http://www.redravengames.com/empires-of-the-void-key-to-the-universe/

As with the other BrainBox games, the object of the game is to study a card for 10 seconds and then answer a question based on the roll of a die. If the question is answered correctly, the card is kept and the person with the most number of cards after 5 or 10 minutes is the winner.

This BrainBox, as the title suggests, is Boston-themed.

Objective: be the first climbing up from a treehouse to a dream house
 - acting by turn -
roll the die (1d6) and move forward on a circuit (containing 20 spaces); throwing a 'joker' (relaces a '6') ==> free choice (1-5); the field you may end on challenge you to:
1. 'house party': pantomine, acting... scoring within 1 min. max.
2. 'sudden inspiration': a given term forces you and the team putzing one word down in writing - scoring if you are matching..
3. 'have a guess': a given question ==> guess how many people will answer with a 'yes'
4. 'cheatery': a story is read aloud and you must jugde if true or not
5. 'big deal': you can score 2 x, but if you fail you loose 1 x
successful? ==> you gain one component; 3 components let you move forward from tree to dream...
remarks:
promotional 'Wüstenrot' (home loan bank) edition with elements out of 'Activity', 'Roller Coaster Hippo'...

Spred is a LOT derivative for Series: Shibumi. It features a simple ball-removal game mechanism that makes loops impossible, but at the same time, produces potentially long games in a very small playing area. An average game lasts 45-50 turns.

Two players (White and Black) start with a supply of 8 red balls each.

The board starts empty. Players take alternate turns placing a ball of their colour (White or Black, not red) on an empty position.

If, after a player's move, there is at least one straight line of three connected* balls (touching) of their colour (the lines can be orthogonal or across levels), the player does the following:

1. Chooses any of those lines of three.
2. Removes the maximum number of movable balls from that line. This means that the player must find out the correct order to remove them so he removes the most. Balls may drop as a result of these removals.
3. Places one RED ball of their supply on any of the empty spaces that composed the removed line.

The game ends when a player runs out of red balls or there are no more available moves, whatever happens first. The player with less red balls in hand wins.

(*)- Note that flat diagonal lines are not connected!

Objective: score most winning points with your team after 5 rounds
 - acting by turn -
active player tries to explain a term by pantomime within 2 minutes - no talking/drawing/writing allowed; 
if successful (somtimes with deduction due to allowed prodedures) there will be a bonus question;

content: 396 cards - splitted into 9 sections, 2 boards "dactylology", 1 card case, Kartenhülle, 1 notepad, rules.
remarks: compareable with 'body talks'

The board game is based on the popular 1985-2000 and 2001-2002 German game show of the same name, for which in turn was adapted from the short-lived 1982-83 American Game Show Child's Play.

A press-your-luck game for two to four fearless poultry fans

by Shawn Storie and Jonathan A. Leistiko

Object: Draw as many points as you can without drawing more than one chicken foot.

You Need:

    A __Chicken__ deck (available on the website)
    A way to keep score (ex: paper and pencil)


Each round is divided into two phases: Bid (you bid how many cards you will draw from the deck — highest bid wins) and Draw (winner of the bid has to draw what he or she bid).

When drawing, the drawing player gets to keep any number cards he or she drew face down in front of them.  If he or she draws any Chicken Feet, the chicken feet must go face up in front of the player.

At the end of the round, the drawing player tallies his or her score. If the player had no chicken feet, the player gets the sum of the points in front of him/her. If the drawing player had one chicken foot, he or she will add an extra 10 points to his/her score. Two or more chicken feet?  The player subtracts the points in front of him/her from his/her score.

If a round ends and at least one player has 50 points or more, the game ends. The player with the most points wins. If any players are tied, play another round with all players.

boardgame: Battle for Liberation: A Handbook for the War in the West 1943-45
description: Battle for Normandy OBs, special rules and Scenarios for company level actions, specifically for the "I Ain't Been Shot Mum!" rules.

A game for two to four pet aficionados.

Goal: - End the game with cats. Lots of cats. Cats that match your personal quirks (youʼre not crazy — just different) are especially nice to have. Donʼt let the feral cat population get to high or youʼll attract the attention of the ASPCA, and you donʼt want that.

You need:
A six-sided die. One die per player is good.
A City deck - this where the cats come from (available on the game's website)
A Thumb-Servants deck - This is where food-bringing servants with opposable thumbs find gifts to bring as offerings to their feline masters (available on the game's website)
Cat Fancier cards - These cards describe the cat fancier youʼre playing in the game(available on the game's website)
Crazy cards - These you what kind of crazy you are. You may have a thing for psycho kitties, lazy fuzzlewumps, or whatever.(available on the game's website)

Each turn begins by determining turn order — the player with the fewest cats goes first in each phase. The players then check to see how hungry the cats are (cats can either be sated, hungry, or famished). Cards from the City deck are then dealt out to the middle of the table. Each player, starting with the first player, then has the opportunity to feed cats, including cats on the table, cats in the Wild that were allowed to go feral in previous turns, cats that are in their household, or other people's cats (which gives them the opportunity to steal those cats). Each player then draws from the Thumb-Servants deck, drawing and keeping cards based on each player's Cat Fancier card. Then the players check to see which cats are hungry, deciding whether to call the ASPCA to pick up the hungry cats (which also may cause the player to lose other cats) or allow the cats to go feral, placing them in the Wild on the table.

The game ends when one or more players has 10 or more cats or if the City deck runs out of cards.

Points are then scored based on the number of cats each player has and any bonuses they may have received from Crazy Cat cards and Cat Fancier cards. However, if the City deck runs out, the ASPCA may come and get everyone's cats - if this happens, everyone loses!

A game for three or more secret-keeping rule enforcers.

Each player gets a secret rule at the start of the game. All rules are in play – including the ones you don’t know. It’s up to the person who has the rule to enforce it or not. Your goal is to be the first player to accumulate 3 points. The best way to do this is by learning the secret rules of the game faster than the other players and taking tricks.

You Need:
A standard Poker deck (Jokers removed)
A Hidden Rules trigger deck (available on the game's website)
A Hidden Rules action deck (available on the game's website)
Paper and pencil to track scores (optional, but recommended)

The basic game is played in tricks. The dealer begins the first trick of the game; after that, each trick begins when the last player to take a trick takes the top card of the deck, places it face-up on the table, and announces its value. Starting with the player to his or her left, and proceeding clockwise, each player must play a card from his or her hand on the trick, calculate the sum of all cards in the trick, and announce the sum.

Card Values: 2 through 10 are worth their face value. Aces are worth 1 or 11 (declared when played). Jacks are worth 0 points, Queens are worth -2 points, and Kings are worth -4 points.

If the sum is less than 21, play continues normally. If you bring the sum to exactly 21, you take the trick. If you push the sum over 21, the player to your right takes the trick, for forcing you to exceed 21. When you take a trick, add one to your score, and gather the cards from the trick into a pile face-down in front of you. Once taken, the cards from a trick may not be looked at.

All hidden rules are also in play. Each combination of a trigger and an action is a rule. It’s up to you to enforce (or not) a rule that you know about. When you enforce a rule, you must give the person information to indicate in what way their action violated the rule. The information you provide must be more specific than the violation. If you violate a rule, take back the card you played (if applicable), lose a point, and your turn ends (if it’s your turn).

For example, trigger #13 says, “When you play a card that makes the total exactly zero…” and action #5 says, “…Name a Peanuts character that hasn’t been named previously this game before you announce the sum of the trick.” If I make the total 0 and don’t say anything, you can make me take back my card and say, “You didn’t say a name.” The next time I make the total 0, If I say, “Neil Armstrong,” you can correct me again and say, “Neil Armstrong isn’t fictional.”

The first player to end a round with 3 or more points more wins.

This game owes a lot to the children’s “folk game” Mao.

boardgame: Birthday Party, Cheesecake, Jelly Bean, Boom!
description:
A very, very lightweight trick-taking game for three to six R.E.M. fans.

Goal: End the game with the most points. Do this by being the last player to play on a trick. Look out for Boom! cards – they’ll make all the points go away!

You Need:


     A Birthday Party, Cheesecake, Jellybean, Boom! (BPCCJBB) deck (available on the game's website)


If you’re starting a trick, you must play a Birthday Party. If you can not start with a Birthday Party, show your hand to the player on your right or left and let play pass to the next player, who must start the trick.

On your turn you may play a card or pass. If you pass on a trick you may still play later on the same trick.

If you want to play a card, look at the top card of the trick:


     If the top card is a Birthday Party, you may play a Birthday Party or a Cheesecake.
     If the top card is a Cheesecake, you may play a Cheesecake or a Jelly Bean.
     If the top card is a Jelly Bean, you may play a Jelly Bean or you may play a Boom! and set all the cards in the trick aside, out of play.


If you run out of cards, immediately draw up to five cards. If the deck runs out of cards, it’s out of cards. No cards for you!

If you play on a trick and play comes all the way around to you and no one else has played on the trick, you take the trick. Put all of the cards in the trick in your score pile.

When you take a trick or Boom! a trick, you must start the next trick.

If it’s the end of a trick and the deck is out of cards, the game is over. Put any cards in your hand in the score pile of the player on your right.

Winning: Sum the points on the cards in your score pile. If you have the most points, you win!

In Whizz Bing Bang, a quick card game for 3 to 6 players, players take turns reading the top card of the deck, with the word on that card dictating how gameplay will continue:


    Whizz: Game play continues in the same direction.
    Bing: Game play reverses direction.
    Bang: Game play continues in the same direction, but skips the next player.
    Boom: Everyone must slap and leave their hand on the Boom while saying "BOOM!".


Penalties for playing out of order, slapping the deck when no Boom was played, being last to hit the Boom card, playing too slowly, etc. are distributed in the form of tokens, with players starting with one face-down token for each sound: Whizz, Bing, Bang, Boom (ordered low to high). The player who is first to hit the Boom card gives a token to the player who hit it last, but in all other penalty situations, players must race to find the lowest face-down token they hold and give it to the penalized player face up; whoever does so first gives the token away while all other players keep theirs and reshuffle them. When one player gives his Boom token away, each player scores one point for each token he holds. When a player has scored 15 or more points, the game ends and the player with the fewest points wins.

Twiga the giraffe and her friends are surprised as all of their brown spots have disappeared and been replaced by spots of various colors and shapes. Is it the mysterious spot disease?

In Twiga, each player has four giraffe cards representing the various parts of his body. By drawing wooden shapes from a bag, players help their giraffe find its real spots; they appear thanks to magic transparent cards. The first player to recover all of his giraffe's spots wins the game!

Objective: score most winning points climbing up the hill (Drachenberg) with your team of 3 (coloured) meeples
 - moving by turn -
roll the die & make up your mind who of your team should move forward; up to your choice: step out, jump or kick out on your way to the top - collecting winning points by adding the position of all your meeples;  
game ends after one figure has reached the peak.

Objective: be the first rendering ice maker machines harmless
 - acting by turn -
use 2 coloured dice to block (evil) ice maker machines;
very similar (risky) concept like 'Can't Stop' moving your markers to the top; if you save your current position ==> new starting points during your next turn; if you fail you loose what you'd accomplished during your current turn.

remarks:
belongs to Schmidt series: 'Nimm-mich-mit-Spiele'

boardgame: Tabaluga: Auf der Suche nach den Weltraumsteinen
description:
Objective: Find your (coloured) 2 (correspondent) slides (memory) and move your meeple upon to gather them; move further to your (coloured) target space ending the game.
 - acting/moving by turn -
Each player throws the die moving forward & uncovering 2 slides:
wrong colour ==> flip them again; right: try (during your following turns) finding the correspondent one. If lucky move upon and grasp it/them (but avoid stepping upon an - additional - quest card, which spoils your further movement).

Munchkin Naughty & Nice, the fourth Christmas-themed Munchkin booster pack, contains 15 non-randomized cards. Now players can use the "Candy Cane" and "Tannenbomb" to fight off the "Snow Angels" and "Holiday Spirits"! "Spike the Egg Nog" to Go Up a Level! Be "Naughty" or "Nice"...or both at once!

The "Naughty" and "Nice" cards have the same text, but different artwork, as the same-named cards in the original Munchkin: Waiting For Santa (which is currently out of print).

Part of the Munchkin series.

In Tabaluga: Der Regenbogenschatz, the objective is to be the first to gather five coloured cards.

For setup, 35 cards (each with 3 different rainbow colours) are laid out on the table. The active player throws 3 dice (each with 6 different colours). Then, all players try to find the matching card. The first person to do so keeps it. But be aware! An already gathered card may be targeted in the next round.

boardgame: A Call To Arms: Star Fleet – Klingon Fleet Box
description:
Publisher Blurb:

The Fleet Boxes are designed for use with any game set in the Star Fleet Universe, including A Call to Arms: Star Fleet, Star Fleet Battles, Federation Commander, and Starmada.  Each contains enough ships for a complete fleet in these games and will allow players to quickly expand their forces.

This box set contains 16 ships for the Klingon Empire, along with 6 shuttles and a pocket rulebook – all you need to start playing!
 - C8 Dreadnought
- D6 Heavy Cruiser x2
- D7 Battlecruiser x2
- C7 Heavy Cruiser x2
- F5 Frigate x3
- D5W Heavy Cruiser
- FD7 Fast Cruiser
- E4 Light Frigate x2
- D5 War Cruiser x2
- Shuttles x6
- Pocket Rulebook
 This product is being done as a joint venture with Amarillo Design Bureau, inc., under the strict terms of their contract with Paramount Pictures Corporation.

A simple dice game where You must complete animals (rooster, dog, cow, pig). 5 dices are rolled, each die face shows one half of an animal - front or rear half. When two halves of the same animal are rolled the player is allowed to grab one of the 10 animal tiles from the middle of the table. If two matching halves are shown then 2 tiles, if none show then he cannot do anything. When no appropriate tiles are in stock a player can snatch one from another player. The game ends when only 1 animal tile is left in the stock. Player with most completed animals wins.

The game consists of large flower tiles in which animals are also shown, and matching flower cards. Besides a memory game, other simple rule sets are also supplied. One is like musical chair: The number of flowers in each color is one less than there are players. The players run around the flowers, and when one player calls out a color, the players have to put their hands on the flowers in that color - the player who is last is out. Continue until only one player remains. 
In another game, a card is flipped over and all players turn up flowers - a player who finds the matching one can keep it and flip over a new card. The first player who gets three flowers wins.

The components of the game are large and suitable for the outdoors.

War Echoes is a 32mm tabletop miniature game, set among a science fiction background. War Echoes uses squad level skirmish combat tactics on an individual character driven system. Five different factions and alien races populate the landscape each with its own unique weapons, abilities and game play style. War Echoes utilizes d10 dice for rolling, with character stamina ratings based on a modifier system for everything from movement to combat.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A 1- or 2(+)-Player sudoku game where players place number tiles to complete up to 100 different puzzles.

From the Rule Book:
For 2 or more players. Game One: Chose a puzzle from the book, place the red number squares on the puzzle board in the same positions as in the puzzle you have selected from the book.  Now take turns -- one move each player -- to try to comlete the game as quickly as possible.  Socring -- if you complete a line either across or down then that player adds up the numbers in that line and that is that players score for that game.  The player with the highest score is the winner after you have completed all nine squares.

For 2 or more players.  Game Two: Set up the numbers in the squares as before.  Player one now turns over the timer and puts on black squares until the timer runs out.  As before add up the numbers in any line that player completes to determine that players score.  When the timer runs out pass on the timer to player 2 who then turns over the timer and when it runs out that player scores as above.  Then pass the timer to the next player who plays the game in the same way and so on depending on the number of players.  When the puzzle is finished then the player with the highest score wins that game.

Tutatis is a fantasy gaelic football game produced by Greebo Miniatures, an italian editor already known for its high quality fantasy miniatures. The game is set in the fantasy world of Triskearth, a brand new world utterly created by the designers of the game. Its history and geography, as well as the background for every race, are described in the first 150 pages of the rulebook. The remaining twenty pages describe the gaming rules, bringing to life a fast & furios game of tactic and bashing!

The game is played on a 11 x 16 squares field, each team can field on the pitch seven payers. Every team (are planned to be released nearly thirty teams) has its own rooster of players, each one with its stats (movement, strength, agility, constitution, ball play), skills and costs, including three different captains. The game time is divided in two halves each one made up of six turns for each player. Turns are played alternatively by each player, completing the action of a single player before passing to the next one. The aim of the game is to score more points (goals or kicks over the crossbar) than the opponent, and you can achieve this with a passing/dodging style or with a more brutal one. Someone could recognize a gameplay similar to other games concerning sports, but this is not a great flaw since Tutatis has it's peculiar flavor thanks to the rules of gaelic football (ie. not forward passage, soloing rule exc.) and to the characterization of each team.

Greebo miniatures has produced a prerelease box of the game (with rules still in beta) at the 2011 Lucca Comics & Games convention, and it was a sold out. That box contained a cardboard field, Italian rulebook, two Greebo's d6, a ball and two teams in 15mm (orcs and dwarves). Since then on Greebo's website have been published in English and in Italian the definitive set of rules and the roosters for the new teams, all available as free downloads. At the moment have been released three kinds of fields (irond peak, thornwell and standard), the miniatures of some mercenaries and nine teams: orcs, dwarves, evil puppets, necros, poffy and five different teams of thornwell (frogs, lizards, turtles, snakes and a mixed team). Greebo's team is working on the championship rules expected to be released as open beta in mid autumn 2012, as well as on a new edition of the game box. It's also on the desk the translation of the rules in french and spanish.

Objective: gather most quiz cards finding correct answers
 - acting by turn -
1. players come to an understanding which side to use (blue or red)
2. depending on no. of players each one is dealt 3-6 cards 
3. active player asks first question - multiple choice (left neighbour); if wrong, next player's turn; if correct, second question is asked;
4. right? hand card over! wrong? next player may answer if he/she bets (an already won) card; wrong? give back risked card - questioned card is taken out, too.
5. game ends after all cards are played.

Object: To be the last player left in the game.
Contents: 36 cards

Before you begin, find 3 tokens (buttons, sweets etc.) for each player. These represent his 3 “lives" and when he has lost them he is out of the game. The pack consists of 4 sets of cards numbered from 1 to 9, which have the following order of importance from highest to lowest:
Farmer's Wife, Dog, Cat, Mouse. Therefore, the 3 of "Dog" beats the 3 of “Cat".

The dealer shuffles the pack and deals one card, face down, to each player. During the game you must try to avoid holding the lowest card. The first player to the dealer's left looks at his card.
He can either say "Stick" and keep it or exchange it for the card belonging to the next player on his left in the hope of getting a higher number and/or set. The next player does the same and so on until it is the dealer's turn. He can either “Stick" or exchange his card for the top one of the pack.
Then all players turn their cards face up and whoever holds the lowest one puts one token into the kitty.
If 2 players hold the same lowest number, the lower set loses. All players put their cards onto a discard pile. The next player becomes the dealer. When the pack has been played through once, pick up the discard pile, shuffle it and play on. Play continues until there is only one player left who wins all the tokens from the kitty.

boardgame: The New York Times Photo Trivia Game
description:
Info from the game box:

Images that shape our times. The most fascinating people. Compelling news stories from the twentieth century.

NEW YORK Photo Trivia is an exciting, challenging trivia game that will test your knowledge of people, places and events that help shape our lives.

Travel around the United States covering the most compelling stories of the last 150 years.

Roll the dice, answer trivia questions and collect the photos that have captured the world's imagination!

Includes:
300 NYT 3 x 5 Photograph Trivia Cards.
Cast Iron Transportation Game Pieces.
Full Color Game Board and 6 sided die.

A Modern Greek word game.

Info from the publisher's website:

You are given the definition of a word, its first letter and what kind of word it is. Find which word it is and beat your opponents while at the same time you are expanding your vocabulary.

Info from the publisher's website in Greek:
Έχεις τον ορισμό μιας λέξης, το αρχικό της και τι μέρος του λόγου είναι. Βρες την, κέρδισε τους αντιπάλους σου, πλούτισε το λεξιλόγιό σου.

Components (info from back of the box):
- Cards with 1800 Greek words
- Cards with 1200 foreign words
- 1 dice
- 6 player pieces
- 1 board
- 1 sheet or rules

A game about Greek proverbs.

Info from the publisher's website:
Game is in Greek. A challenging game with 580 proverbs, sayings and mottos. By playing it the players enrich their vocabulary, learn the meaning and the proper use of proverbs while having fun. They understand when "A miss is as good as a mile" why "Adversity makes a strange bedfellow" and why "A friend to all is a friend to none".

Info from the publisher's website in Greek:
Ανταγωνιστικό παιχνίδι με 580 παροιμίες, εκφράσεις, ρητά και λαϊκά γνωμικά. Παίζοντας, οι παίκτες εμπλουτίζουν το λεξιλόγιό τους, διδάσκονται την έννοια και τη χρηστικότητα των παροιμιών, ενώ παράλληλα ψυχαγωγούνται. Κατανοούν πότε έζωσαν κάποιον τα φίδια, σε ποια περίπτωση πέταξε το πουλάκι, τι σημαίνει του έδωσε τα παπούτσια στο χέρι, σε ποιον ταιριάζει ο νοών νοείτω, για ποιον λέμε ο πνιγμένος απ’ τα μαλλιά του πιάνεται...

boardgame: Ποιός Πού Πότε Γιατί?  για παιδιά
description:
A children's version of Ποιός Πού Πότε Γιατί?.

A Greek trivia game for primary school children, 2400 questions about Geography, History, Arts, Animals and Plants, Sports and Science. The players move around a board and try to reach the finish line first.

(An earlier edition seems to have 1500 questions, a newer one 2400.)

Greek description:
Η παιδική έκδοση του Ποιός Πού Πότε Γιατί?.

Ένα ελληνικό παιχνίδι για τους μικρούς μας φίλους, με 2400 ερωτήσεις Γεωγραφίας, Ιστορίας, Τέχνης, Ζώων και Φυτών, Αθλητισμού και Επιστήμης. Οι παίκτες κινούνται στο ταμπλό και προσπαθούν να φτάσουν πρώτοι τον τερματισμό.

Contents: 36 cards in 6 sets of six.
Object of the Game: To collect six identical cards.

Sort out one set of 6 identical characters per player. Place other cards out of the game. Shuffle and deal six cards face down to each player. The game consists of exchanging cards as quickly as possible. Each player looks at his cards, decides which one he wishes to exchange and places it face down in front of the player to his left. Each player then picks up the card placed in front of him and adds it to his hand before deciding which card to exchange next. To keep play running smoothly, the players should simultaneously say “Pass” and "Pick Up". The first player to get six identical cards and shout "OINK" is declared the winner.

Trivia game about Germany.
The game consists of 240 questions in the categories Town and Country, Art and Culture, History and Modern Times, Food and Drinks, Sports, Leisure and Holiday as well as Demands, 5 questions and 1 demand per cards, in varying sequences. Correct answers earn souvenirs, demands must be fulfilled when possible and change ownership of souvenirs. One can only hold a limited number of souvenirs of a kind, or must exchange them. Whoever has the most souvenirs at the end of the game wins.

A Hungarian football-themed trivia game. Players answer questions and move their player pieces on a board.

Components: 1 game board, 8 player pieces, 800 cards with 4800 questions, 1 die, game rules

BUILD a party of specialized fantasy heroes.

CONSTRUCT a battlefield with environment tiles, terrain, and fortifications.

DESTROY your opponent(s) with a mix of melee, ranged, and magical attacks.

TactDecks: Reign of Heroes is a fully customizable, expandable battle system. A single set supports 2 players, or add additional sets to increase the size of your battlefield and options.

Highly portable and adaptable, TactDecks supports a creative combination of battlefield layout and party configurations. Whatever your tactical preference - spell, sword, arrow, subterfuge, or a little bit of everything - TactDecks adapts to your tactical style.

There's no arenas. No static mats. Movement is organic and possible in every conceivable direction (including up and down). Climb, move, or demolish terrain, don't just look at it!

Objective: be the first scoring as many goals as agreed (3 or more) 
 - acting simultaniously/ 2 players or 2 teams (red and blue) -
game board has to be prepared (each row - 6 in total - is filled up with 5 slides/different numbers)
ball must be moved (from the attacker) into the next closer row in direction of defender's keeper: attacker nominates 2 (of 11) meeples with hidden numbers ==> multiply both to move ball upon a matching slide/area; defender acts in a similar way; if successful (same result) defender becomes attacker; if not, attacker gets closer to the goal ==> additional turn (with less meeples on both sides).
Close enough to score? - use goal & keeper chips in a similar way.

Any number can play. All the 36 cards are dealt face down to the players. Each player keeps his cards, without looking at them, face down in front of him. The player on the left of the dealer starts the game by turning up his top card and placing it face up in the centre of the table.
When a “PUT" card (marked with numbers 1,2 or 3) is turned up the next player has to put down the number of cards shown. If in doing so he turns up a “PUT" card, the next player has to put down the required number.
Should no “PUT” card turn up in the cards laid down, the player who laid the “PUT” card takes all from the centre of the table.
The player who gets all the cards wins the game.

Some 'R' Set is a trick-taking game that uses a unique deck of 28 cards. The card suits are fractions (ex. 0/0, 0/1, 1/1, 0/2, 1/2, 2/2, ..., 0/3,..., 0/4, ..., 0/,6, ... 6/6) with the mid-cards also being worth extra points.

Some 'R' Set is best played with partners as in Spades. The object is to bid on how many points you and your partner will take. There are 21 available points: 14 from the available tricks and 7 from the red cards. Bidding goes around the table once where the players bid for the two-card blind. High bid takes the blind and decides which suit (fraction set) will be trump.

As in many trick taking games, and if you make your hand, you get the points; if you do not, you are set, hence the name: Some "r" set.

In the 2020s, publisher The Levi Game Company marketed this item as Single Some 'R' Set to distinguish it from Double Some 'R' Set, which (as of 2025) it is marketing under the Some 'R' Set. Confusing, yes...

Can you avoid three cats and dispose of all your cards.

The cards are actually called Guckloch-Karten and are triangle shaped (to represent a slice of cheese). Further more each card has six 'faces'. Each 'face' will either be a cat, or a mouse, or a hole. The holes mean that when it is placed on a stack, the 'faces' on the card underneath the holes will show through.

The game is played in rounds and a round ends when one player has no cards left.

Each player gets a stack of cards. The board is plastic and represents a cheese. In a turn a player takes the top card of his stack and chooses which side to play. Then he lays the card in the sliced area of the board. Then the next player does the same, and so on.

The object is to lay a card so that at least 3 mouse faces are visible. If this is acheived, the player can play another card. And so on, but no more than 3 cards can be played in a single turn. Should a player play a card that has three cat faces shown, that player must take up all the cards played so far and put them at the bottom of his stack. If you use your last card, you win the round and receive a cheese token.

The first player to get three cheese tokens, wins.

Game description from the publisher:

It is 1947, and the armies of the three great blocs have collided in a small city along the Caspian Sea. Zverograd, a city that lies at the divide of continents, lies also at a crossroads in world history. Now, the battle that has raged here for months has reached its tipping point, and only through the use of recently developed technological terrors and innovative new tactics can any of the three warring factions prevail.
 Dust Warfare: Campaign Book Hades, a supplement for Dust Warfare, continues the tense struggle for this all-important metropolis, while adding new rules, options, and stats for upcoming Dust Warfare units. This 48-page softcover book has details on locations around Zverograd, rules for Tesla weapons, and new special abilities. What's more, this jam-packed resource features the epic Hades Campaign, a long-term conflict in which players plan their engagements using a detailed overland map of Zverograd.

The game consists of 30 treasure chest tiles which also show a jewel in one of six colors, and six large wooden pirate meeples. 
The tiles are laid out in a 6 x 5 grid and a pirate is placed on a tile of each color, covering the jewel so it can no longer be seen. The active player now rolls a color die and has to find the jewel in the right color (by picking up the pirate). If correct, the player gets the tile and places the pirate on another tile in that color. If wrong, the pirate is put back onto the tile. The game ends when only three colors are left; the player with most tiles is the winner.

The Realm of Agennul is an ever evolving board game where you control the fate of the characters and the future of the realms. Our goal is to create a fantasy world and outlet for participants to collaborate and decide how characters, realms, and the lore develop within the game.

Players receive two characters, which must work together to earn the patronage of cities within Agennul. During the game players must protect those cities from invading players who are also competing for the control of the Realm.

Use characters to navigate through the Realm and conquer cities to gain coin and control of Agennul. With the coin upgrade, increase your city's defenses by paying coin for a fortification or purchase equipment for your characters to increase their abilities. Engage in epic battles between players as ego's grow while the limited towns and cities are fought over. Be conniving and pay off players to avoid conflict in order to secure your advancement in the Realm.

The Realm of Agennul is very simplistic at its core but can develop into a complicated strategic game as power shifts around the table between players.

The first player to have control of the most cities as well as the patronage of the Hero's City of Anos, is anointed as The Hero of Agennul and Protector of the Realms.

Following the success of Pillow-Dex, Parker released a more sophisticated (and more expensive) version. With the balloons, this version also has a cord net which you could tie between two chairs, and rackets. Based on the indoor parlor scene on the box, Parker intended for this to be a game for indoors.

The game has 2 tops with spinners, and 10 small wooden pins that look sort of like bowling pins. The box bottom has four wooden pegs affixed in the pattern of a square in the center of the box. The eight pins are setup, and then each player in turn spins his top. 5 points for knocking a pin down, 10 points for throwing it out of the box. 500 points wins the game.

Daze of Our Loans is a game for anyone with a sense of humor.  The players are "lenders" that move around the board making loans and, sometimes, recovering on them.  With luck they may even obtain collateral. When circumstances warrant, loans can be sold or participated out to others.  Meanwhile, the economy may quickly change from normal times to recession or boom, sharply changing your loan portfolio values and affecting your strategy.  Players must also deal with unpredictable court judgments, costly regulatory inquiries, bankruptcies, and documentation errors, among other common celebrations in a lender's day.

The game lasts for one hour, then all players get 2 more turns.  The player with the most money wins.
Simple race game where each of fours players race to get their three pegs from start to home. Spinner will give values between 1 and 4 for each spin.

This is a simple children's game. At the start of each game each player takes his color marker's and covers up all the positions on his fourth of the board. When a player spins a number then he has to lift a marker that has that number under the marker. If another number is exposed, then the marker is returned. The first player to remove all his markers wins.

Since there is a combination of memory and luck, this would be an interesting game for an adult to play with a child.

The game has markers for each player and a spinner with the values 1-6. From the game board:

Directions for the game on "Tiger Tom" No 4660

Tiger tom is lost. Who will find him? Each player chooses the side he wishes to play on, and the one who spins the highest number starts the game. The spaces between the cross marks count one. The player has to follow the black line until he reaches the center where he will find Tiger Tom. The one who arrives there first finds Tiger Tom and wins the game. If a player lands on No. 1 by exact spin, he must go back and start again. If a player lands on any of the black spots by exact spin, he must go back five spaces. If he lands on a red spot, by exact spin, he may advance three spaces. A player may pass another but if he lands on the same space, he must go back to where he started and lose one turn. If four wish to play, two start from each side.

Traps is a marble shooting game where each player tries to shoot marbles into specific locations on a board. When opened the game box creates a ramp, and a "backstop." One the backstop are seven position which have a hinged flap that shuts when a marble is shot through the opening. from left to right the positions are numbered 1 to 7. The positions also have point values of 5, 15, 10, 25, 10, 15, and 5. Three games can be played on the board, Traps, Golf and Tournament Play. From the directions:

HOW TO PLAY TRAPS

Any number of players may compete. Each player in turn will roll or shoot an inning of four balls, scoring the points as they are made, the one first reaching a total of five hundred points becoming the winner of the game.

HOW TO PLAY GOLF

TRAPS now becomes a seven hole links. Drive for No. 1 hole: after holeing, recover ball, return trap to closed position and shoot for No. 2; continue for the entire course. Player making seven holes in fewest shots is declared the winner.

Tournmment - Twenty-Five Bird Event

Player will specify by small number (not one or seven) the Pigeon he expects to break; if succesful, 1 is scored and player shoots again, first designating the intended shot (not one or seven); if again succesful, another point is credited and the third shot may be either 1 or seven, but in no case may one or seven be called, unless the shooter has previously broken two consecutive birds other than one or seven. failure to break the bird designated, loses the players turn, which goes to each entrant in rotation; winner is the one who first breaks 25 birds.

This is a simple chase and capture game in that the Giant is trying to catch Jack. The Giant is a large pawn shaped piece, and Jack is a small disc. There is a spinner with values of 1 to 5. There are interconnected paths with dots to mark positions along the paths.  The game board is glued to the bottom of the two piece box.

Directions (from game board)

Jack the small piece starts from home, the Giant from Castle. Spin in turn, each player moving as many dots as spun over any route, either forward or backward. They cannot pass ecah other on the same path. If Giant lands on dot occupied by Jack, he wins. If Jack reaches Door of Giants Castle and gets home safely, he wins (having got the hen that lays the golden egg).
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Jack and the Beanstalk

description:
Winter has come. There's a brief cease-fire. The factions begun preparations for the next stage of war, a brutal clash that will come in a few months. In the meantime, though, they are reinforcing their troops. Thanks to the new resources found near the gigantic Frozen City, new leaders are preparing for spring. Winter sleep is, in the end, just a cover for a secret arms race...

Winter, an expansion pack for both 51st State and The New Era, introduces new rules and 54 new cards to both games that will reward gamers who prefer sustainable development over unpredictable aggression.

Winter lasts six weeks – i.e., six rounds – and during that time players prepare for the war that will take place after winter. Each round players can send their workers to Frozen City, a special mini board, to hire mercenaries and find new leaders. With Winter, the aim of the game has changed; you no longer fight for 30 victory points (VPs), developing in a rush to hit that total and win before the other player. Now you have a precise amount of time in which to plan and build, in which to develop your faction.

Winter introduces a new Texas faction that's crowded with people (four workers in the production phase) and has the possibility of using one of its faction contacts twice in a round.

Came with four (4) wooden "bean bag" discs, a target board (with holes marked 5 points, 10 points and 20 points) and a stick with which to prop up the target board.

Directions from top of box:

    Arrange the Game as shown in the Picture below. The players
   pitch in turns, the first one who scores 50 points wins the
   game.

   Manufactured by J. H. Singer 
   New York, U. S. A.

This is sort of a "Go Fish" type of card game, but three cards, out of the 105 cards in the game, are needed to make a book. Sets are determined by the day of the week upon which the historical event took place. From the rules:

        4273
           Home History Game
           For Boys and Girls
    Copyright 1909 By Milton Bradley

This game consists of 105 cards, each bearing a historical event and date, including the day and week upon which such event took place. The dealer in the first game is chosen by lot, and thereafter the deal goes in turn to the left. Shuffle the cards and deal one at a time beginning at the left until ten have been dealt to each player. The remainder are placed in a pile face down on the table. The player at the left of the dealer commences the game by making as many "books" as he can from his hand. Three cards of the same day constitute a book. These books are laid aside to be counted at the end of the game. The player now calls upon any player he wishes for any card (any day of the week) he, the caller, may need to complete a book. If successful, he may continue to call for and receive the needed cards from any of the players, until he calls for a card from a person who has not that card; he then draws three cards from the pile and the next player plays.
   
The players follow in turn at the left from the first player. At the end the player having secured the greatest number of books wins.

A simple race game with 17 spaces on the board. The spinner has 8 different positions. So this is a very simple spin and move game. There are 4 markers that come with the game.

Spinner positions:

     3 - Land
     3 - Take off or Fly
     1 - Fly
     1 - Bad weather go back to last city (ie lose a space)

Checkers played with 2 or 3 players on a six-sided hex board, with 61 black hexagons touching only at their points, not their sides, leaving small red spaces between, and forming a larger overall hexagon.  Each player picks a side separate from other players.  Complexity comes not as much from the board itself but from the addition of an extra player's pieces.

Other 3 player Checkers variants have instead stretched a Checkers board's squares into a "Y" or triangular shape.

The publisher's name suggests there may have been a Chess variant published with a similarly styled board.

A simple and short game for young children.
"Get as high as you can and get the gold. But watch out - if you climb high it's a long fall."
Players climb up the dragon by rolling dies, and try to get gold pieces from the dragon. But if they get to high they fall down and have to start over again. When all the gold have been delt, the player with the most pieces wins.

Among the Stars: Ambassadorial Shuttle is a promotional Special Location card set available at the Artipia Games booth at Spiel 2012.

The card set includes two copies of the Ambassadorial Shuttle card and a third card printed with the relevant rules.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Two gangs of bikers meet in the desert and after drinking themselves silly decide that jousting on their motorbikes, using shotguns rather than lances, is a good idea.

A two player push your luck game. Each player has 11 cards, and each selects and plays one card from their hand simultaneously until one or both players plays the "BLAM" card, which ends the round.  The player playing the "BLAM" card scores for the cards they have already played this round. The game is played over four rounds.

The third installment of the Touhou Shisouroku, a re-theme of the popular Dominion card game.

This expansion features 16 new kingdom cards taken from Intrigue, Hinterlands, Prosperity and a few from other expansions.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable
family: Video Game Theme: Project Shrine Maiden

description:
From the Rulebook:

The Space Fleet Patrol army book requires the Star Confrontation rules book in order to play.  Space Fleet has highly trained Fleet officers that can deal with almost any situation and their ability to teleport around the battlefield means they will always be in the right place at the right time.  Their advanced space shttles use their powerful pulsars to decimate the enemy.

A simple timed game where players name items in categories such as "Things you don't want to step on barefoot".
 In turn, players draw a card, then read the category on the card and the amount of time out loud (from 5 to 15 seconds).  The player then names as many things that fit the category as possible in the allotted time.  The player gets one point for each named item.

After each player has three turns, the highest score wins.

From the rule book:
"Counting Town is an engaging, imaginative and colourful play set that invites the child to do something very special: learn while playing."

The rule book provides 12 games for children to play.  Each game is provided as a learning excercise for the children playing.  The games focus on the language and number concepts such as colours, distance, time, quantity, sequencing etc.

All 12 games follow the same sequence of play:
The teacher setups the board for play.
A background story is provided for the game.
The teacher poses questions to the children based on the board setup.
The children then take turns based on the directions of the teacher (i.e.  the teacher directs the children to move the parts around the board - the teacher then asks additional question to the children).

From the rule-book:

The object of all blistergames is to win by being the last player or team, with one or more active blisterpieces on the board.

The game is played with dice.  There are two styles of dice:  the blisterpieces (which have a different symbol on each die face) and the blisterblockers

The rule book provides six variations of the game:  blisterboxes, blisterangles, blistercrosses, blistertriangles, blisterpokes and blisterballs.

Depending on the variation in play the blisterpieces are oriented such that they all have the same symbol showing.  The symbol in play determines the movement that is allowed for that particular die (i.e. Blisterangles must move horizontally and vertically, Blisterpokes can move either horizontally or vertically).

The sequence of gameplay is as follows:
Player 1 rolls the 8-side die.  
Player 1 moves one piece (die) the number rolled ensuring they follow the movement rules determined by the symbol on the die.
If the die ends its move within 6 squares with direct line of sight a "Blistermatch" may be started.  During a Blistermatch the two dice being disputed is fought over.  If one player can form the take-out pattern (identified in the rules) the losing die is eliminated.  If a Blistermatch reaches a stalemate it ends and the game resumes as normal.
Gameplay continues with alternating turns until one player eliminates the other players pieces or a stale mate occurs.

Naruil Airfoil Championship is a steampunk airship racing game where players compete in a race of skill and madness. Each year a grand championship is held where teams from all over the realms come to put their skills to the test.  The race begins with each team (player) receiving an old airfoil frame that will form the basis for their airship in the race.

The teams get four hours (turns) to fix up and prepare their ship for the race.  When the time is up the teams must roll out their ship no matter the condition.. The race is on!  The teams will race around the designated track, trying to stay ahead of the pack as they face challenges along the way.  The airfoil that is the first to successfully cross the finish line, after the designated number of laps, wins.  This is a rough race and you never know which racer, if any, will pull through in the end!

In honor of the 150th anniversary of the Battle of Gettysburg, military professionals, historians, and game designers created this game to closely replicate the actual battlefield conditions and operations of Gettysburg in 1863. The game comes in both a standard and collector’s edition and is played on an oversized game board with 172 miniature commanders and 126 brigade counters. The collector’s edition comes with life-like pewter miniatures on a magnetized metal game board for stability, and a carved wood base with drawers for storing the miniatures.
 In lieu of the game’s dice and chart system, attacking and defending units can enter their strength, location, and orientation into the Interactive Game App (IGA) which then simulates the battle conditions to determine results. A unit’s strength is represented by possible Combat Factors on a brigade counter encircling a rotating miniature. The miniature is turned toward lower numbers as casualties are taken or is increased up to full strength as depleted units are merged into it. Reinforcement, Supply, and Morale Cards also affect the Combat Factor and randomness of game play.

The game also uses a Double Turn Rule where after the initial attack, both the attacker and defender may move all of their units one hexagon, defenders first. This allows both sides to retreat, stand, or bring up reinforcements during the turn. Units still in contact fight each other one more time before the turn ends. Artillery has a range of four hexagons and is only required to engage in battle if the enemy’s artillery is in range.

Incremental casualties, merging and resupplying units, and double-turn combat combine to recreate actual conditions, while the IGA cuts playing time in half. Tactical skill, not luck prevails, and the winner may rewrite history.
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description: This board game for adult on a hen do or in another party or good natured fun setting. Each player takes a coloured playing piece and works their way around the board's main track (red) and sub-tracks (childhood, adolescence, middle age and old age) until you reach the twilight zone. First to finish with a hand of 20 scores wins.
